segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a serene night scene featuring a large, glowing full moon rising over a dark landscape with silhouetted trees, accompanied by mysterious, low synth music as a male voice asks, "Do you want to see what the world is really like?" and another responds, "Yes." The scene quickly shifts to a vibrant, colorful animated world where a blonde woman drives a pink convertible through a sunny, palm-tree-lined suburban street, as a deeper male voice reflects, "I find the world a complicated place." This transitions to a tense, dimly lit interior where a man in a red vest appears to be in a confrontation, while a countdown begins with "Three." A dramatic shot then shows a man in a dark coat standing atop a futuristic, illuminated structure against a dark sky, as the voice continues, "We imagine a future," and the countdown reaches "Two." The sequence cuts to a group of armored figures walking through a fiery, war-torn environment, emphasizing danger and conflict, as the voice concludes, "And our imaginings horrify us," followed by "One" and an explosive sound effect. A close-up of a man in a suit at a table, possibly in a tense negotiation, is then seen as a female voice urgently shouts, "Stop it!" This is followed by a surreal, animated explosion of red energy in a dark room, suggesting a supernatural or magical event, while an assertive male voice declares, "There's gonna be some changes made." The mood shifts to warmth and intimacy with a close-up of a smiling couple, as a softer male voice expresses, "I love people so much." A dramatic, animated scene of a man in a red and white outfit carrying a child through a lush, sunlit landscape unfolds, while the softer male voice continues, "It's hard for me to be alone," followed by a loud, almost animalistic roar/scream. The video then shows a man in a white shirt lying on a bed, seemingly in distress, as a concerned female voice asks, "Anita, you here?" This cuts to a group of people joyfully splashing in water, conveying liberation and celebration, as a reassuring male voice says, "People wanna love you." A man stands triumphantly on a snowy mountain peak, arms raised, symbolizing victory or achievement, as the reassuring male voice advises, "You should let 'em love all of you." The tone darkens again with a black-and-white shot of a woman in a vintage setting, looking concerned, as an authoritative male voice states, "Humans only have one ending." A chaotic, neon-lit cityscape with people running in panic, possibly during an explosion or disaster, is shown as a determined female voice asserts, "Ideas live forever." A dramatic, dark scene shows a figure falling through a stormy sky, as an encouraging female voice begins, "Imagine knowing that you could do something..." followed by a close-up of a woman in a white dress with a hand gently touching her shoulder, suggesting comfort or connection, as the encouraging female voice finishes, "...that only you could do." The final frames depict a woman in a futuristic, mechanical headpiece, possibly undergoing a transformation or experiment, as a deep, impactful male voice declares, "The world will remember this day." The sequence ends with a man in a dark suit standing in a dimly lit room, surrounded by glowing objects, hinting at a mysterious or technological plot, as a calm female voice instructs, "Close your eyes. Now, feel."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a tense, dramatic orchestral score accompanying a dimly lit scene of a man in a brown shirt and a woman in a patterned shawl seated at a table. The music then softens as the scene shifts to a bright outdoor setting where a blonde woman in a pink sequined outfit and white cowboy hat appears emotional, a tear rolling down her cheek, before she smiles warmly while sitting on a blue bench with a man in a black tuxedo. A male voice remarks, "You're so beautiful," to which the man in the tuxedo, Jimmy Kimmel, replies, "I know. I was just thinking that." He then humorously adds, "I haven't eaten in three weeks. I'm so hungry. I have to go host the Oscars." The atmosphere dramatically transforms as upbeat, fanfare-like orchestral music swells, accompanied by enthusiastic crowd cheering and applause. An announcer's voice declares, "Live from the Dolby Theatre in Hollywood, please welcome tonight's host, Jimmy Kimmel!" Kimmel, now confidently walking onto the grand, golden-lit Oscars stage with 'OSCARS' prominently displayed, greets the formally dressed audience, saying, "Thank you, thank you, thank you. Hi everybody," and "Wow, it's very kind. Thank you. Thank you very much," as the audience continues to clap and cheer, celebrating his poised presence.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Oscars Host, a man in a sharp black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing confidently on a grand stage adorned with golden, flowing drapes. He smiles warmly and gestures expressively with his hands, engaging the audience with animated body language. His voice is heard, thanking the audience for a "partial standing ovation" and welcoming everyone to the "96th Oscars." The camera alternates between close-ups of his face and gestures and wider shots that reveal the full scale of the Oscars Stage, featuring a circular platform illuminated by soft white light and a large golden Oscar statue in the background. The word 'OSCARS' is prominently displayed above the stage in elegant lettering. The host continues, praising the "beautiful human actors" and expressing his honor to be there, congratulating attendees for making it to the Academy Awards and "on time." The audience, visible in wide-angle shots, is seated in a large, ornate theater, dressed formally and clapping enthusiastically, with some laughter heard in response to the host's jokes about the show starting early but ending late, and already being "five minutes over." The atmosphere is celebratory and glamorous, with warm golden lighting enhancing the luxurious setting. The host continues to speak with charm and poise, using open hand gestures to emphasize his words, reflecting on the past year as "hard" but "great for movies," and acknowledging the "enormous talent and untold potential" in the room, which elicits more audience laughter after a humorous comparison to "Madame Web." He then playfully asks, "Are we off to a bumpy start?"

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing confidently on the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, adorned with drapes and featuring a large Oscar statue. He gestures expressively, welcoming the audience, and begins his monologue, stating, "This is a meaningful occasion for most of you... winning an Oscar is something you dreamed about since you were a kid." He continues, "And now here we are, all dressed up, celebrating the best of the best, beginning with the biggest movie of the year, Barbie." The camera pulls back to show the circular stage and the word 'OSCARS' above, as the Oscars Audience, formally dressed, erupts in enthusiastic clapping and cheers. Close-ups show a blonde woman in a sparkling gold dress and another in a dark sequined gown smiling and applauding. The host, with a comedic tone, declares Barbie a "monster hit" and "what an achievement to take a plastic doll nobody even liked anymore," joking about his wife's previous disinterest in Barbies. He then credits Greta Gerwig for making Barbie a "feminist icon," noting that many believed she "deserved to be nominated for Best Director," which elicits more applause and cheers from the audience. The host playfully retorts, "I know you're clapping, but you're the ones who didn't vote for her by the way. Don't act like you had nothing to do with this," drawing laughter. He concludes by acknowledging Margot Robbie for putting the "giant hit together," as the audience continues to clap, maintaining the celebratory and glamorous atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Oscars Audience, showcasing a group of formally dressed attendees, including a woman in a shimmering black strapless gown and a man in a black sequined jacket, both smiling and clapping as the sound of applause fills the air. The camera then cuts to the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing confidently on the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, which features flowing drapes and warm lighting. He gestures expressively as he speaks with a clear, engaging voice, announcing, "Margot Robbie and Ryan Gosling are here tonight. Look kids, it's Barbie and Ken sitting just near each other." He continues, addressing them directly, "even if neither one of you wins an Oscar tonight, you've both already won something much more important, the genetic lottery," which elicits widespread laughter from the audience. The host, maintaining his charm and poise, then playfully adds, "Ryan, you are so hot. Let's go camping together and not tell our wives," drawing more laughter. The camera alternates between the host and the attentive, entertained audience, which includes notable figures like a woman in a gold sequined dress and a man in a tuxedo with gray hair, both smiling and clapping. The host continues his monologue, using hand gestures to emphasize his points, transitioning to mention "the other major box office winner this year, Oppenheimer, directed by the great Christopher Nolan," prompting applause. He describes Nolan as "also a very attractive man," leading to more laughter, and then shares fascinating details about Nolan's work habits, including writing scripts on a computer with no internet connection, concluding with a humorous punchline about not letting "porn addiction get in the way of my work," which again sparks widespread audience laughter. The atmosphere is celebratory and glamorous, with the golden stage and elegant attire contributing to the grandeur of the event.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Oscars Audience, where a man in a tuxedo and a woman beside him are laughing heartily, their amusement clearly audible. The scene quickly cuts to the Oscars Stage, a grand, golden-lit setting with flowing drapes, a circular platform, and a large Oscar statue, where the Oscars Host, dressed in a sharp black tuxedo with a bow tie, stands confidently. He speaks with a clear, engaging voice, introducing Cillian Murphy as a "long-time collaborator" and "just wonderful," then humorously explaining the pronunciation of his name as "Killyan" for drama and "Sillyanne" for comedy, eliciting hearty laughter from the audience. He congratulates "Sillyanne's" co-star, Robert Downey Jr., whose name is met with applause. The host declares this a "highest point" in Robert Downey Jr.'s "long and illustrious career," then playfully corrects himself to "one of the highest points," prompting more laughter. The camera alternates between the host, who gestures expressively with animated hand movements, and the diverse, elegantly dressed audience, who clap and react with visible excitement, including a man with a gold pin smiling warmly and another in glasses clapping and pointing playfully. The host continues his monologue, making a joke about a "drug motion" and then praising Robert Downey Jr. as "so handsome, so talented," and having "won every award there is to win," before humorously asking if he has an "acceptance speech" in his pocket, which is met with further laughter and enthusiastic applause, creating a dynamic and joyful atmosphere throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Oscars audience, focusing on a man in a black tuxedo with glasses and a black shirt, seated next to a smiling woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress. The camera then cuts to the grand Oscars Stage, where the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, stands confidently on a circular platform amidst golden drapes and a large Oscar statue. He speaks with charm and poise, gesturing toward the audience, asking, "Do you just have a very rectangular penis?" which elicits laughter from the engaged audience. He continues his monologue, recalling a movie where "Tim Allen turns into a dog," before introducing a surprising guest: a black-and-white dog named Messi, wearing a bow tie, sitting attentively in the audience. The host praises Messi's "performance of the year in Anatomy of a Fall," specifically mentioning an "overdose scene," and jokes about not seeing a French actor "eat vomit like that since Gerard Depardieu," drawing more laughter and applause. The camera alternates between the host, who occasionally places his hand on his chest, and the audience, capturing reactions including a woman clapping and a man in a white tuxedo smiling. The host then announces "Poor Things," directed by Yorgos Lanthimos, as the second most nominated movie, prompting enthusiastic applause and cheers from the crowd, maintaining a lively and celebratory atmosphere.

The scene then cuts to the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing confidently on the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, which features flowing drapes and a large Oscar statue. He gestures expressively with his hands, engaging the audience with charm and humor, noting the presence of "two Yorgoses" among the nominees, which elicits laughter and applause from the audience. He then playfully asks for "20 seconds for room tone," drawing more laughs. The host continues, praising Emma Stone for her fifth Oscar nomination and her "unbelievably great" performance in "Poor Things," humorously comparing her character to "the lady who gave the rebuttal to the State of the Union," prompting significant laughter and clapping from the audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress. The camera alternates between the animated host and the reacting audience, capturing the Woman in White Dress smiling warmly. The host then shifts to a comedic critique of this year's films, stating, "Your movies were too long this year," and revealing that the average length of top 10 movies was "2 hours and 23 minutes," up 30 minutes from three years ago, maintaining a lively and polished presence under the warm, theatrical lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, confidently delivering a comedic monologue on the grand Oscars Stage, which is adorned with golden drapes and features a large Oscar statue in the background. He gestures expressively with his hands as he jokes, "When I went to see Killers of the Flower Moon, I had my mail forwarded to the theater," and adds, "Killers of the Flower Moon is so long, in the time it takes you to watch it, you could drive to Oklahoma and solve the murders yourself," eliciting laughter and applause from the audience. The camera then cuts to a wide shot, revealing the full scale of the stage with 'OSCARS' illuminated above, and a large, formally dressed audience seated in the auditorium. The host continues his dynamic monologue, engaging the crowd with animated hand movements and a warm smile, introducing "the multi-talented Bradley Cooper" and noting his "best picture nominee with Maestro," which is met with enthusiastic applause. He then playfully addresses Bradley Cooper, asking if he brought his mom, and jokes about how many times one can bring their mom as a date before it becomes "actually dating his mom," prompting hearty laughter from the audience. The camera alternates between close-ups of the host and reactions from the Oscars Audience, including a Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, a Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, and a Man in White Tuxedo, all clapping and smiling. A notable moment captures a Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress wearing large, sparkly sunglasses, who laughs heartily at the host's jokes. The host points toward the audience, drawing attention to specific guests, including a man in a tuxedo seated next to the woman in sunglasses, before sharing some "fun Oscar trivia" about Robert De Niro and Jodie Foster's nomination for Taxi Driver 48 years ago. The atmosphere is lively and celebratory, enhanced by golden lighting, as the host maintains eye contact and uses expressive gestures to emphasize his words. The segment concludes with the host smiling broadly, hands clasped, as the audience continues to react with enthusiasm and applause.

segments.integrated_caption: The Oscars Host, a charismatic man in a black tuxedo, stands confidently on the golden-draped Oscars Stage, with a large Oscar statue gleaming behind him. He engages the audience with expressive hand gestures, delivering a monologue that elicits frequent applause and laughter. He jokes about past nominations, referencing 1976, and makes a humorous remark about Jodie Foster and Robert De Niro's age difference, drawing chuckles from the crowd. The camera frequently cuts to the Oscars Audience, showing various attendees reacting enthusiastically; the Blonde Woman in Pink Car, now in a navy blue satin dress, claps and beams, while the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with sparkly sunglasses laughs heartily. Other audience members, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress and the Woman in White Dress, are seen smiling and clapping warmly, and a woman with striking blue gemstone jewelry nods appreciatively. The host then announces Lily Gladstone's nomination as the first Native American ever nominated for Best Actress, specifically for 'Killers of the Flower Moon,' which is met with significant applause. He continues with a witty anecdote about her almost quitting acting to track 'murder hornets,' concluding with a joke about his worry over who is tracking them now, prompting more laughter. The atmosphere remains celebratory and glamorous, with the golden lighting and formal attire complementing the host's commanding yet approachable presence and the audience's joyful engagement.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, confidently speaking and gesturing expressively on the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, which features flowing drapes and a large, illuminated Oscar statue in the background, with the word 'OSCARS' prominently displayed above. He announces, "Lily is an excellent company. We have many first-time acting nominees tonight," proceeding to list names including Emily Blunt, Jeffrey Wright, Sterling K. Brown, America Ferrera, Danielle Brooks, Colman Domingo, Da'Vine Joy Randolph, and Sandra Hüller. The camera occasionally cuts to the Oscars Audience, who react with smiles and applause, their cheers growing louder as the host mentions that "for the first time ever, three foreign language films are up for Best Picture, and two of them star Sandra Hüller." The Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress is seen clapping warmly, seated next to the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, who also smiles and engages. The host continues, detailing Sandra Hüller's roles in "Anatomy of a Fall" and "The Zone of Interest," humorously remarking that these "very heavy subjects for American moviegoers" are "rom-coms" in Germany, eliciting hearty laughter from the audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses. The host, with animated hand movements, occasionally pointing or spreading his arms wide, then reveals that "for the first time in more than two decades, we're adding a new category to the Oscars," clarifying "Not tonight, don't worry. In the future, they will be adding..." The warm golden stage lighting emphasizes the grandeur of the event as the host maintains his engaging and theatrical demeanor.

segments.integrated_caption: The Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, stands confidently on the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, which features flowing drapes and a large Oscar statue. He gestures expressively as he announces "An Oscar for achievement in casting," prompting immediate applause from the Oscars Audience. He then delivers a joke about actors winning awards for parts others didn't get, eliciting laughter, particularly from the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses who laughs heartily. The host continues, reflecting on a tough year, before directly addressing "Stephen," the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, who is seen smiling warmly and clapping next to the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress. The host asks Stephen if he's nominated or has season tickets, then acknowledges Stephen and his wife, Kate Capshaw, for their significant donations to help actors and writers during the recent 148-day strike. Throughout his animated monologue, the host maintains eye contact, occasionally clasping his hands, while the audience remains engaged, with the Woman with Blue Gemstone Jewelry nodding appreciatively, all bathed in the warm, celebratory golden light of the event.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing confidently on the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, which features flowing drapes and the word 'OSCARS' prominently displayed above a large golden Oscar statue. He gestures expressively with his hands, conveying charm and poise, as he delivers a comedic monologue, stating, "not the directors. You guys folded immediately. But the rest of us said we will not accept a deal without protections against artificial intelligence." His facial expressions shift between smiles and thoughtful pauses as he continues, "actors no longer have to worry about getting replaced by AI. Thanks to this historic agreement, actors are now able to go back to worrying about being replaced by younger, more attractive people. And I think that's great." This elicits hearty laughter and applause from the formally dressed Oscars Audience, visible in wide-angle shots. The camera occasionally cuts to audience reactions, including a Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses who laughs heartily, and a Woman with Blue Gemstone Jewelry who smiles and nods appreciatively. The host continues, asking, "And writers, could AI have written Transformers Rise of the Beasts? Yes, the answer is yes," drawing more laughter. He then reflects on the strike, saying, "This strike raised existential questions about our industry like if a movie premieres at the Grove and there are no actors there to promote it, does Mario Lopez make a sound?" which again prompts significant audience laughter. As the host occasionally clasps his hands or spreads his arms wide, engaging the crowd with dynamic energy, he concludes, "And now that the strike is over, now that Fran Drescher has returned to her volunteer work reading loudly to the hearing impaired, we can be proud of the fact that this long and difficult work stoppage taught us that this very strange town of ours as pretentious..." The atmosphere remains celebratory and polished, with the golden tones of the stage and lighting creating a luxurious, cinematic feel, punctuated by the host's performance and the audience's enthusiastic responses.

The segment opens with a wide shot of the grand Oscars Stage, illuminated by golden drapes and a large Oscar statue, as the Oscars Audience stands, applauding enthusiastically and cheering. The camera pans across the energetic crowd, capturing individuals like the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses laughing heartily, the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses smiling, and the Woman with Blue Gemstone Jewelry nodding appreciatively. The Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, steps onto the circular stage, clapping and gesturing warmly. He speaks with charm and poise, engaging the audience with expressive hand movements, his clear voice heard joking about making the show 'really long tonight so you get a ton of overtime,' eliciting laughter from the crowd. He then asks, 'Should we give out some Oscars?' to more cheers, before announcing 'We're gonna kick it off with the Oscar for Actress in a Supporting Role.' As orchestral music begins to swell, the scene transitions to a presentation of the Supporting Actress nominees, with large vertical screens displaying five women holding their Oscars—each in distinct, elegant gowns. A formal male voice announces, 'The Oscar goes to...' followed by a rapid succession of names: 'Mary Steenburgen,' 'Lupita Nyong'o,' 'Jamie Lee Curtis,' 'Rita Moreno,' and 'Regina King,' presumably from past wins. The audience continues to applaud as the nominees are shown, and the camera captures the stage’s dynamic lighting and grandeur. The segment concludes with the nominees walking onto the stage, greeted by continued applause and the swelling orchestral music, as the host steps aside to make room for the presentation.

segments.integrated_caption: The grand Oscars Stage, illuminated with golden drapes and a large circular platform, sets the scene as a woman with silver hair in a sleek black dress stands center stage, speaking with poise and emotion. Her voice, clear and warm, announces, “I am thrilled to be back on this stage to present this year's Best Supporting Actress Oscar,” adding that she is “beyond proud to be able to talk about one of my besties.” The camera pans to a woman in a sparkling silver gown standing to the side, then cuts to a wide shot displaying five large vertical screens, each showcasing a “Supporting Actress Nominee” holding an Oscar and dressed in elegant gowns of varying colors and styles, from black floral to sparkling silver. The speaker returns to focus, elaborating on Jodie Foster's role in “Nyad,” describing her as bringing “physical intensity, coupled with deep concern and care as Bonnie Stoll, to Diana Nyad's fierce best friend, motivator, and serious ass-kicker.” She adds, “speaking from personal experience, all of those qualities are very real about Jodie as well. There is no one you would rather have your back than the multi-talented Jodie Foster.” As she speaks, the scene transitions to the Oscars Audience, where a blonde woman in a navy blue satin dress (the “Blonde Woman in Pink Car”) smiles warmly and claps enthusiastically, seated next to a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress, also smiling and clapping. The camera alternates between the speaker on stage and the engaged audience, capturing their emotional reactions, with a split-screen effect emphasizing the connection. The formal, celebratory, and emotionally charged atmosphere is punctuated by the audience's enthusiastic applause and cheers, highlighting the significance of the moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the grand Oscars Stage, featuring a golden backdrop, flowing drapes, and a large Oscar statue, as four elegantly dressed women stand on a circular platform, clapping along with the audience. The auditorium is filled with enthusiastic applause. The camera then focuses on a woman in a black floral dress with a prominent gold buckle, who speaks warmly and emotionally, gesturing with her hands. She recounts a powerful moment from 'Oppenheimer' involving Emily Blunt's character, Kitty Bissering Oppenheimer, refusing to shake a betrayer's hand, and praises Emily Blunt's extraordinary performance and depth, expressing her pride to be Emily's friend. As she speaks, the scene cuts to the engaged audience, including a woman in a sparkling silver dress with a diamond choker, seated between a smiling man in a white tuxedo and another in a black tuxedo, all reacting with smiles and attentiveness. The camera alternates between the animated speaker on stage and the appreciative audience, sometimes using a split-screen to emphasize their connection. Following more applause, the camera pulls back to reveal the words 'SUPPORTING ACTRESS' illuminated above the stage. The segment concludes with a close-up of a woman in a dazzling silver beaded gown and glasses, smiling confidently on stage, who then begins to speak, introducing 'Mary.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Woman in Silver Beaded Gown, wearing a dazzling silver beaded gown with a deep V-neck and glasses, standing confidently on stage. Her expression shifts from composed to joyful as her voice is heard delivering a heartfelt speech, praising a performance as a tribute to those who have helped others heal and to a grandmother whose glasses were worn in a film, calling it an honor to see the world through her eyes. The scene transitions to a split-screen view, showing her on the left and the Blonde Woman in Pink Car on the right, who is now visibly emotional, wiping tears from her eyes while seated in the audience, reacting to the moving words. Loud applause erupts from the audience. The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the grand Oscars Stage, revealing a golden backdrop with a large Oscar statue, where four women stand: the Woman in Silver Beaded Gown, the Woman in Black Floral Dress, the Woman in Black Dress on Stage, and the Woman in Red Gown. The focus shifts to the Woman in Black Dress on Stage, dressed in a dramatic black ruffled gown, leaning forward with expressive gestures as her powerful voice exclaims "America!" twice, drawing out the syllables. The audience, including the Blonde Woman in Pink Car and the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, reacts with enthusiastic applause and smiles. The camera returns to the Woman in Black Dress on Stage, capturing her animated delivery and emotional intensity as she continues, stating that a "powerful Barbie monologue is perhaps the most talked about moment in the most talked about movie of the..." before the audio cuts off, with the atmosphere remaining one of celebration, emotion, and high drama, underscored by the opulent stage design and the audience’s engaged reactions.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen view of the Oscars audience, featuring a woman in a shimmering pink sequined dress and a woman in a dramatic black ruffled gown, who is seen leaning forward with expressive, animated gestures. The scene transitions to the grand Oscars Stage, where the woman in the black ruffled gown is now speaking with intense, clear, and emotional delivery, addressing the audience about 'impossible standards females must try to live up to.' She stands alongside three other women: one in a black floral gown, another in a sparkling silver dress, and a third in a vibrant red one-shoulder gown. As the speaker concludes her powerful tribute with 'congratulations on your tour de force,' the camera focuses on the woman in the red gown, who places a hand over her heart, smiling emotionally, while the audience erupts in applause and cheers. The video cuts back to the audience, showing the woman in the red gown now seated, flanked by a man in a white tuxedo and a woman in a red sequined dress, all reacting with smiles and enthusiastic applause. The speaker then announces, 'Danielle Brooks,' prompting a surge of louder applause and cheers. The scene returns to the stage, where Danielle Brooks, the woman in the red gown, speaks with poise, as the speaker continues to recount her journey from seeing 'The Color Purple' at 15 to her Broadway debut as Sofia. The video concludes with a split-screen showing Danielle Brooks on stage and a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress in the audience, both smiling and engaged in the celebratory and emotionally charged atmosphere, bathed in warm lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress and the Woman in Red Gown, seated in the audience, smiling as the Woman in Black Dress on Stage delivers a heartfelt speech, stating, "10 years after that, giving this transcendent performance on the big screen. We don't just see Sophia's strength, but we see her vulnerability as well. And you, you have earned this full circle moment, my friend." The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, displaying 'SUPPORTING ACTRESS'. The Woman in Black Dress on Stage, holding an envelope, continues her speech before dramatically announcing, "And the Oscar goes to Da'Vine Joy Randolph!" The audience erupts in thunderous applause and cheers as a graphic lists the nominees. Jodie Foster, the Blonde Woman in Pink Car, is visibly emotional, wiping tears, while the Woman in Sparkling Silver Dress and the Woman in Pink Sequined Dress clap enthusiastically. Da’Vine Joy Randolph, radiant in a shimmering light-blue gown, rises from her seat to triumphant, celebratory background music. A male voice-over announces, "This is the first Oscar win and nomination for Da'Vine Joy Randolph." She walks to the stage, is greeted by the Woman in Black Dress on Stage who hands her the Oscar statuette, which Da’Vine Joy Randolph embraces with a broad smile. The camera follows her as she walks back through the still-applauding audience, passing the warmly clapping Woman in Red Gown, all bathed in golden lighting, creating a celebratory and emotional atmosphere.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a close-up of two women in the audience: the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, wearing a black strapless gown with vertical silver embellishments and a pearl choker, and the Woman in Red Gown, in a vibrant red one-shoulder beaded gown with a high neckline. They are seated together, smiling and reacting to the ceremony. The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the Oscars Stage, a grand golden-lit space with flowing drapes and a circular platform, where the category 'SUPPORTING ACTRESS' is displayed in large letters above the stage. The Woman in Black Dress on Stage, with short gray hair and wearing a sleek black dress, stands at the podium holding an envelope. A graphic appears on screen listing the nominees: Jodie Foster, Emily Blunt, Da’Vine Joy Randolph, America Ferrera, and Danielle Brooks, each shown in a circular frame with their name below. The Woman in Black Dress on Stage announces the winner, and the audience erupts in applause. The camera pans across the audience, capturing reactions: the Blonde Woman in Pink Car (now identified as Jodie Foster) in a navy blue satin dress, visibly emotional and wiping tears; the Woman in Sparkling Silver Dress, smiling and clapping; and the Woman in Pink Sequined Dress, also clapping. The focus shifts to Da’Vine Joy Randolph, who is seen in a shimmering light-blue gown with voluminous ruffled shoulders, rising from her seat and walking toward the stage with a joyful expression. She is greeted by the Woman in Black Dress on Stage, who hands her the Oscar statuette. Da’Vine Joy Randolph embraces the award, smiling broadly, as the audience continues to applaud. The camera follows her as she walks back through the audience, passing the Woman in Red Gown, who claps warmly. The atmosphere is celebratory and emotional, with golden lighting and a sense of triumph filling the grand auditorium.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, where the Oscars Audience is seen enthusiastically clapping. Da’Vine Joy Randolph, resplendent in a shimmering light-blue gown with voluminous, ruffled shoulders, walks toward the podium amidst the sustained applause. As she reaches the microphone, the camera zooms in for a close-up, revealing her emotional expression with tears welling in her eyes. The golden drapes behind her provide a warm backdrop, and on-screen text identifies her as 'DA’VINE JOY RANDOLPH, ACTRESS IN A SUPPORTING ROLE, THE HOLDOVERS.' Her voice, trembling with heartfelt emotion, begins with a repeated, grateful declaration of "God is so good." She continues, sharing, "I didn't think I was supposed to be doing this as a career," recalling how she started as a singer and how her mother guided her to the theater department, stating, "There's something for you there." Da'Vine expresses profound gratitude, thanking her mother and "all the people who have stepped in my path and has been there for me, who has ushered me and guided me," adding, "I am so grateful to all you beautiful people out here." Throughout her speech, she gestures occasionally with her hands, her blonde hair styled in soft waves, and subtle sparkling earrings visible, as the camera alternates between close-ups of her face and wider shots, with the audience's applause continuing softly in the background, creating an atmosphere of celebration and deep personal significance.

As she reaches the microphone, the camera zooms in for a close-up, capturing her emotional expression—tears welling in her eyes as she begins her acceptance speech. The golden drapes behind her create a warm, radiant backdrop. On-screen text identifies her as 'DA’VINE JOY RANDOLPH, ACTRESS IN A SUPPORTING ROLE, THE HOLDOVERS.' The camera alternates between close-ups of her face and wider shots showing her standing alone at the podium, with the audience’s applause continuing in the background. Her blonde hair is styled in soft waves, and she wears subtle, sparkling earrings. The atmosphere is one of celebration and deep personal significance as she acknowledges her achievement.

segments.integrated_caption: On the grand, golden-lit Oscars Stage, adorned with flowing drapes and a large golden Oscar statue, Da’Vine Joy Randolph, visibly moved and tearful in a shimmering light-blue gown with voluminous ruffled shoulders, delivers a deeply emotional acceptance speech into the microphone. Her voice trembles as she powerfully declares, "I just need to be myself," recalling a past moment when she was "the only Black girl in that class" and was told, "you told me I was enough." The camera captures close-ups of her emotional face and wider shots revealing other women on stage, including those in black floral, sparkling silver, black off-the-shoulder, red, and black ruffled gowns, standing respectfully and clapping amidst the warm, dramatic lighting. Da'Vine expresses profound gratitude to "all the women who have been by my side," specifically naming Colleen Camp, Barbara Broccoli, Tracy Brennan, and Sarah Fargo, before humorously and emphatically thanking her publicist, stating, "You don't have a publicist like I have a publicist." Her hands occasionally gesture as she concludes, "I am forever grateful. I pray to God that I get to do this more than once," her words met with sustained applause, encapsulating an atmosphere of celebration and heartfelt emotion.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment captures a powerful and emotional moment on the Oscars Stage, where Da’Vine Joy Randolph, wearing a shimmering light-blue gown with voluminous ruffled shoulders, delivers her acceptance speech at the podium. Her expression is deeply moved, with tears in her eyes and a trembling voice, as she speaks into the microphone. The camera alternates between close-ups of her face, highlighting her emotional state, and wider shots that reveal the grand golden backdrop of the stage with flowing drapes and a large golden Oscar statue in the background. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Da’Vine Joy Randolph, in a shimmering light-blue gown with voluminous ruffled shoulders, delivering an emotional and tearful acceptance speech at the Oscars podium, her voice trembling as she says, "I thank you for seeing me. Have a blessed night. Thank you so much." As the audience erupts in loud, sustained applause, accompanied by upbeat orchestral music, the camera pulls back to reveal the grand Oscars Stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue and other nominees beside her, including a woman in a black floral dress, a woman in a teal gown, a woman in a sparkling silver dress, a woman in a black dress with a metallic skirt, and a woman in a white gown with a high slit. Close-ups show the Woman in Pink Sequined Dress smiling broadly and the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses laughing heartily. The scene then shifts to the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, who steps onto the stage with a warm, engaging demeanor, gesturing expressively as he begins his monologue, congratulating Da'Vine Joy Randolph on her Oscar and her "very sweet, beautiful speech," humorously adding, "You know you forgot to mention your publicist's name. I think your publicist needs a publicist is what it is." He continues with a story about Da'Vine learning to smoke for her role in 'The Holdovers,' joking that her director mailed her fake cigarettes, but she ended up smoking real ones, concluding with a laugh, "You see kids, sometimes smoking is fine!" The audience responds with laughter and applause. The camera cuts back to Da’Vine Joy Randolph, now seated in the audience, holding her Oscar and clapping, her face beaming with joy and pride. The host continues his performance, using animated hand gestures and a confident smile, while the audience, including the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses and the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, react with amusement and appreciation. The warm, golden lighting emphasizes the celebratory and glamorous atmosphere, capturing the emotional high of the award win and the host’s charismatic presence.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, delivering a humorous monologue on stage, engaging the audience with expressive hand gestures and animated facial expressions. He jokes about giving out "silver Oscars for second place" and asks about letting kids fill out ballots, eliciting laughter and reactions from the Oscars Audience. The camera captures close-ups of his dynamic delivery before pulling back to reveal the grand Oscars Stage, illuminated with warm golden and red lighting, featuring the prominent 'OSCARS' sign above a circular platform. The host then enthusiastically introduces "the God of Thunder and Princess Peach, Chris Hemsworth and Anya Taylor-Joy," as the audience erupts in applause and cheers. The stage is flanked by large vertical screens that sequentially display vibrant, animated clips from various nominated films, including characters like a blue fish, a red-haired girl, a green monster, and a young girl in a green dress, creating a colorful and celebratory montage set to whimsical orchestral music. A voiceover declares, "The greatest artists can come from anywhere," followed by a child's voice exclaiming "Wow!" and stating, "It's all it is, a leap of faith." As the montage concludes, the screens transition to a red-lit backdrop with arched columns, and two presenters—Chris Hemsworth, the Man in Dark Suit, and Anya Taylor-Joy, the Woman in Silver Beaded Gown—walk onto the stage to continued applause. Anya Taylor-Joy joins Chris Hemsworth at the podium, and they prepare to announce an award, with the audience visible in the foreground, clapping and reacting to the presentation, all underscored by grand, celebratory orchestral music. The atmosphere is formal, energetic, and celebratory, emphasizing the glamour and excitement of the Academy Awards ceremony.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens on a grand Oscars stage, adorned with red and gold art-deco designs, as a man in a black tuxedo and a woman in a sparkling silver gown stand at a podium. The male presenter's voice announces, "Here are the nominees for best animated feature," while four arched screens behind them display animated characters from the nominated films: a robot and dog from 'Robot Dreams', a boy with a sword from 'The Boy and the Heron', a fiery elemental character from 'Elemental', and Spider-Man from 'Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse'. The presentation transitions into a dynamic montage of clips from each nominated film, showcasing diverse animation styles from hand-drawn to 3D. As the clips play, the male presenter reads out the titles and names of the creators: 'The Boy and the Heron' (Hayao Miyazaki and Toshio Suzuki), 'Elemental' (Peter Sohn and Denise Ream), 'Nimona' (Nick Bruno, Troy Quane, Karen Ryan, and Julie Zachary), 'Robot Dreams' (Pablo Berger, Ibon Cormenzana, Ignasi Estapé, and Sandra Tapia Díaz), and 'Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse' (Kemp Powers, Justin K. Thompson, Phil Lord, Christopher Miller, and Amy Pascal). The montage, accompanied by celebratory background music and applause, culminates with the on-screen title 'ANIMATED FEATURE FILM'. Returning to the stage, the female presenter, with a cheerful tone, exclaims, "Alright, and the Oscar goes to... The Boy and the Heron," as the audience erupts in applause. A woman in a shimmering light-pink gown then walks forward to accept the Oscar, holding it with both hands. A male voice, accepting on behalf of the winners, states, "The Boy and the Heron's Hayao Miyazaki and Toshio Suzuki cannot be here with us tonight, so we accept this Oscar on their behalf," followed by the female presenter's "Congratulations!" The presenters applaud as the woman steps onto the stage, with the audience clapping in the background. The stage lighting remains warm and celebratory, now featuring the boy character from 'The Boy and the Heron' flanking the presenters on the backdrop.

The presentation transitions into a montage of clips from each nominated film, with on-screen titles identifying them: 'The Boy and the Heron' (Hayao Miyazaki and Toshio Suzuki), 'Elemental' (Peter Sohn, Denise Ream, Karen Ryan, Julie Zackary), 'Nimona' (Nick Bruno, Troy Quane, Karen Ryan, Julie Zackary), 'Robot Dreams' (Pablo Berger, Ibon Cormenzana, Ignasi Estapé, Sandra Tapia Díaz), and 'Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse' (Kemp Powers, Justin K. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat orchestral score and enthusiastic applause, showcasing the grand Oscars stage adorned with art-deco red and gold columns and arched screens displaying animated characters from 'The Boy and the Heron', 'Elemental', 'Nimona', 'Robot Dreams', and 'Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse', all under the 'ANIMATED FEATURE' category. The audience, including a woman in a white dress, a man in a dark suit with glowing objects, and a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress, claps enthusiastically. The scene transitions to the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, who steps onto the stage with a confident smile. He delivers a comedic monologue, joking about 'The Boy and the Heron' nominees not being present and the 'Adapted Screenplay' category, eliciting laughter from the engaged audience, which includes a man in a tuxedo with glasses and a woman with blue gemstone jewelry. The host then introduces "two-time Oscar nominee Melissa McCarthy and Oscar winner Octavia Spencer," prompting loud applause and cheers from the audience as upbeat orchestral music swells. Melissa McCarthy, in a black sequined blazer and black pants, and Octavia Spencer, in a vibrant red gown with a magenta cape, walk onto the dramatically lit purple and white stage. Octavia Spencer, holding an envelope, stands at the podium and speaks with a warm, calm voice about original and adapted screenplays being "cut from the same cloth," while Melissa McCarthy listens attentively, occasionally gesturing.

segments.integrated_caption: On a grand stage with soft, curved white and purple backdrops, a woman in a black sequined blazer with flared sleeves over a black dress gestures expressively with her red-painted nails as she speaks into the microphone, stating, "Yet, they remain unique. Yet, each art form maintains its individual characteristics. It's kind of like us, right? I mean, we do our own things, we also do our things together, we're like the Chippendale of showbiz." Beside her, a woman in a vibrant red gown with a dramatic magenta cape and puffed sleeves holds a beige envelope labeled 'ORIGINAL SCREENPLAY' with a gold seal. She listens attentively, occasionally glancing at the envelope and smiling warmly, her large diamond earrings and rings sparkling under the stage lights. The woman in black continues, "I don't see us as the like repant pelvic thrust types," to which the woman in red playfully retorts, "Well, speak for yourself." Their interaction escalates into a humorous debate over the pronunciation of "Chippendale" versus "Chip-in-Dale," with both women speaking with clear, engaging voices, punctuated by laughter. The woman in black then suggests, "Why don't we just read what the brilliant writers wrote for us?" The woman in red exclaims, "Oh! Look! There's a script on the thing," as the woman in black adds, "And it has my name up there. Look at it." The woman in red observes, "Oh, it's in yellow," as they continue their warm and lighthearted presentation, enhancing the celebratory and polished atmosphere.
segments.visual_original_caption: On a grand stage with soft, curved white and purple backdrops, two women stand at a microphone, presenting an award. The woman on the left, wearing a black sequined blazer with flared sleeves over a black dress, gestures expressively with her hands as she speaks, her red-painted nails catching the light. The woman on the right, dressed in a vibrant red gown with a dramatic magenta cape featuring puffed sleeves, holds a beige envelope labeled 'ORIGINAL SCREENPLAY' with a gold seal. She listens attentively, occasionally glancing at the envelope and smiling warmly, her large diamond earrings and rings sparkling under the stage lights. The two interact playfully and warmly, sharing glances and laughter, suggesting a lighthearted and engaging presentation. The atmosphere is celebratory and polished, with the stage lighting enhancing the elegance of their attire and the significance of the moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, with long dark hair and red-painted nails, gesturing expressively at a podium on a stage with a soft purple and white backdrop. She speaks, saying, "To make a script really come to life, the writer must not only capture the audience's hearts, but simultaneously entertain and surprise." She then adds, "Wow. That is better when it's written better. It's better written. Much better. It's better written, guys. Much better written." Beside her, the Woman in Red Gown with Magenta Cape, wearing a vibrant red dress with puffed sleeves, holds a beige envelope labeled 'ORIGINAL SCREENPLAY' with a gold seal, smiling and nodding in agreement. The Woman in Black Sequined Blazer then announces, "Here are the nominees for Best Original Screenplay." The scene transitions to a series of title cards on crumpled paper, each introducing a film: 'ANATOMY OF A FALL' by Justine Triet and Arthur Harari, followed by a clip of Sandra, a woman in a purple shirt, speaking with intensity, "You are not a victim. Not at all. Your generosity conceals something dirtier and meaner." Next, 'THE HOLDOVERS' by David Hemingson is shown, with a clip of Kountze, a young man in a brown blazer, saying, "I can't fail this," to which Paul, a man with a mustache in a bow tie, responds, "Oh, don't sell yourself short, Mr. Kountze. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Grace, a blonde woman in large orange sunglasses and a white collared shirt, speaking in a sunlit outdoor setting, stating, "Insecure people are very dangerous, aren't they? I'm secure." The scene transitions to a title card reading 'PAST LIVES' with 'Written by Celine Song' beneath it, as a voice announces, "Past Lives, written by Celine Song." Next, Hae Sung, a man in a blue shirt, sits with a woman in a light-colored blouse in front of a glowing carousel, with his voice heard saying in Korean and English, "You disappeared from my life, and I just found you again." The scene then shifts to an awards ceremony, where a Woman in Red Gown with Magenta Cape stands at a podium with a Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, who announces, "And the Oscar goes to... Anatomy of a Fall. Justine Triet and Arthur Harari." The audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress and the Man in Dark Suit, erupts in applause as celebratory music plays. Jodie Foster, in a black pinstriped sequined suit, rises from her seat, hugging a man in a dark suit before walking down the aisle, waving and smiling amidst clapping guests. She reaches the grand stage, with its circular lighting and draped purple fabric, where she is handed the Oscar by the presenters. The Man in Dark Suit on stage adds, "This is the first Oscar win for Justine Triet and Arthur Harari. Justine Triet is also nominated for directing Anatomy of a Fall." Jodie Foster stands beside him, both holding their awards, as the audience continues to cheer, and she briefly says, "Thank you. Well, okay," in a celebratory and emotional atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: On the grand Oscars stage, illuminated with warm lighting and featuring a circular design against draped purple fabric, Justine Triet, in a black pinstriped sequined suit, holds her Oscar and speaks into the microphone, visibly emotional with tears in her eyes and a red lip. She expresses profound gratitude, humorously stating the award will help her through her 'midlife crisis' and describing the 'crazy year.' She contrasts the current glamour with being 'stuck in a house with two kids' during lockdown, resorting to cartoons for peace, and concluding there's 'no line... between work and diapers,' eliciting laughter from the audience. Beside her, Arthur Harari, in a black suit, holds his Oscar, listening intently and displaying his own emotion. He then takes the microphone, acknowledging producers Marie-Ange Luciani and David Thion, and humorously likens their creative process to 'mixed doubles ping-pong played by neurotic French nerds,' also drawing laughter. The camera alternates between close-ups of their expressive faces and wider shots, while a lower-third graphic identifies them and their Original Screenplay award. In the background, attendees, including a woman in a red gown with a magenta cape and another in a black sequined blazer, clap and smile, contributing to the celebratory and heartfelt atmosphere, punctuated by continuous audience applause and laughter.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Arthur Harari, in a black suit, expressively speaking into the microphone with a French accent, holding his golden statuette alongside Justine Triet, who listens intently in her black pinstriped sequined suit. Arthur emphasizes the "total independence and freedom" vital for their film, drawing applause. The camera cuts to a close-up of Arthur, then to Justine, visibly moved, stepping forward to speak. With red lipstick and blonde hair catching the lights, she emotionally thanks the cast, including Sandra, Swann Arlaud, and Antoine, for "killing the script on set," concluding with heartfelt "Thank you so much" as the audience, including Jodie Foster, applauds. Upbeat, celebratory music swells as the pair celebrate with raised arms against a backdrop of typewriters, and the camera pans to diverse, clapping audience members. The scene transitions to the 'ADAPTED SCREENPLAY' award, presented by Da’Vine Joy Randolph, in a black sequined blazer, who playfully says, "Oh, it's me. It's you. Here are the nominees for Adapted Screenplay," alongside a woman in a red gown with a magenta cape. The woman in the red gown then announces 'AMERICAN FICTION' as the winner, crediting Cord Jefferson, as a clip from the film shows a man on a city street and people walking in a suburban neighborhood, maintaining the celebratory and emotional atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a somber, introspective conversation in a dimly lit interior, where Monk, a bald man with a beard and glasses, wearing a white shirt, asks Cliff, a man with short black hair and a beard, also in a white shirt, "Does seeing a dead body ever become normal?" Cliff responds, "I don't know. I haven't seen many," and after Monk's surprised "Really?", clarifies, "I'm a plastic surgeon. If I'm looking at a corpse, then something went very awry." An announcer's voice then introduces a title card for 'THE ZONE OF INTEREST,' written by Jonathan Glazer and based on a novel by Martin Amis. The scene transitions to a suburban setting where a child rides a toy car on a gravel path, as Linna asks Hedwig, "Jews in the house?" Hedwig replies in German, "Die Juden sind da drüben hinter der Mauer. Das sind Mädchen aus dem Dorf," (The Jews are over there behind the wall. They are girls from the village) and instructs children, "Sag Oma guten Tag" (Say hello to Grandma). The announcer's voice returns for the 'OPPENHEIMER' title card, written by Christopher Nolan and based on the book "American Prometheus: The Triumph and Tragedy of J. Robert Oppenheimer" by Kai Bird and Martin J. Sherwin, followed by a massive explosion sound as a group of men crouch behind a wooden barrier in the desert. In a dimly lit room, Bohr, a man in a dark suit, speaks calmly, "Algebra's like sheet music. The important thing isn't can you read music, it's can you hear it. Can you hear the music, Robert?" to which another voice replies, "Yes, I can." Finally, an announcer's voice presents the 'BARBIE' title card, written by Greta Gerwig & Noah Baumbach and based on Barbie by Mattel, before cutting to a vibrant, pink-hued room where Gloria, in a pink polka-dot blouse and pink pants, expresses her exhaustion, "I'm just so tired of watching myself and every single thing I do."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Gloria, a woman in a pink polka-dot blouse and pink pants, standing in a vibrant pink room with abstract, swirling patterns. She speaks emotionally, her voice clear as she expresses her exhaustion with women tying themselves into knots to gain approval, stating, "single other woman tie herself into knots so that people will like us." A distinct 'whoosh' sound accompanies the transition to a title card for 'POOR THINGS,' crediting Tony McNamara for the screenplay and Alasdair Gray for the novel. This is followed by a dramatic outdoor scene where Max, in a brown coat and top hat, earnestly tells Bella, in a yellow coat, "It is your body, Bella Baxter, yours to give freely." Bella pragmatically responds with a clear voice, "I generally charged thirty francs," to which Max replies, "Well, that seems low." The scene then cuts to a formal awards ceremony, where a woman in a black sequined blazer, her voice clear and resonant, announces, "And the Oscar goes to... American Fiction!" alongside a woman in a red gown with a magenta cape holding an envelope. The Oscars audience erupts in applause and cheers as a man in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, identified by a male announcer as Cord Jefferson, the winner, rises from his seat. He walks down the aisle, smiling and waving, surrounded by clapping guests, receiving congratulations and high-fives. The announcer adds, "This is the first Oscar win and second nomination this year for Cord Jefferson, who is also nominated as part of the producing team of American Fiction." The joyous atmosphere is underscored by continuous applause and swelling background music as he makes his triumphant walk to the stage.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic shot of Cord Jefferson, a bald man in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, walking down the aisle of a grand auditorium to the sound of upbeat, celebratory orchestral music and enthusiastic applause from the formally dressed Oscars Audience. A woman in a vibrant red gown with a magenta cape embraces him warmly before stepping aside. As he reaches the circular Oscars Stage under bright lights, holding a golden Oscar statuette and a card, the music fades slightly, and he begins his acceptance speech, saying, "Oh, thank you so much. Thank you so much." On-screen text identifies him as 'CORD JEFFERSON, ADAPTED SCREENPLAY, AMERICAN FICTION.' He continues, "My God, uh, look, this is thank you so much, thank you so much," as applause continues. He speaks with passion and an earnest, slightly emotional voice, gesturing expressively, stating, "This really, this means the world to me. Thank you so much to the Academy. Um, thank you so much to everybody who worked on the film." He then reflects, "I've been talking a lot about how many people passed on this movie in discussing it and I worry that that sometimes sounds vindictive, and I don't want to be vindictive, I'm not a vindictive person anymore. I've worked very hard to not be vindictive anymore." He clarifies, "And it's more a plea. It's a plea to acknowledge and recognize that there are so many people out there who want the opportunity that I was given." The audience remains engaged, clapping and laughing as he humorously interjects, "I only got six seconds, please. Uh, I understand that this is a risk..." The warm lighting emphasizes the significance of the moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Cord Jefferson, a bald man in a black tuxedo with a black bow tie and a red rose boutonniere, standing at the Oscars podium, holding an Oscar statuette and a card. He delivers an emotional and expressive acceptance speech, gesturing with his free hand while occasionally glancing at the card. He passionately advocates for taking risks on smaller films, stating that "$200 million movies are also a risk" and suggesting making "20 $10 million movies" or "50 $4 million movies" instead. He expresses immense joy at being there and making his film, hoping others experience that joy, and proclaims that the next Martin Scorsese, Greta (both Gretas), and Christopher Nolan are "out there" just needing a shot. He thanks those who trusted "a 40-year-old black guy who'd never directed anything before," emphasizing how it changed his life. The stage is illuminated with warm lighting and features draped purple fabric, creating a grand and celebratory atmosphere. The camera cuts to the diverse audience in formal attire, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, and the Woman in Sparkling Silver Dress, who clap and react with joy and excitement, their applause and cheers punctuating his speech. The Woman in Red Gown with Magenta Cape is seen embracing Cord Jefferson warmly as he walks to the stage, then standing beside him, smiling and clapping along with the rest of the audience. The camera returns to Cord Jefferson, who continues his speech with passion, raising the Oscar and card for emphasis amidst the sustained applause. The audience’s enthusiastic reactions are captured in close-ups, highlighting their emotional engagement. As he concludes, triumphant, celebratory music swells, accompanying the roaring applause and cheers. The segment concludes with a brief, abrupt cut to a woman with long dark hair, wearing a black-and-white checkered jacket over a white blouse, standing in front of a vibrant pink background, speaking into a microphone—this appears to be a new, unrelated scene, contrasting sharply with the formal Oscars setting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a grand, dimly lit auditorium bathed in purple and pink hues, filled with an enthusiastically clapping and cheering audience. The camera pans across the crowd, capturing close-ups of various attendees, including Jodie Foster, who is visibly emotional, wiping tears from her eyes, and later smiling warmly. On stage, under a circular, illuminated backdrop, an announcer states, "Please welcome Dwayne Johnson and Bad Bunny." The Rock, in a shiny silver suit with a maroon scarf and silver chain, walks confidently to the center alongside Bad Bunny, who wears a black double-breasted suit with glasses and holds a card labeled 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE'. The Rock delivers a speech in English, stating, "Cinema is a universal language that speaks to the common threads that bind us together." Bad Bunny then adds in Spanish, "El arte del cine es un lenguaje universal que habla a los hilos que nos unen juntos," before translating to English, "Beautifully said. You see, he is not such a bad bunny. Happy birthday, brother. Gracias, thank you." He continues, "International films enrich our cultural understanding by broadening our perspectives of the world and each other. Here are the nominees for International Feature Film." The camera cuts to a wide shot of the stage under a large 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE' sign as the audience continues to applaud. The video then shifts to a montage of clips from nominated films, each introduced by an announcer: "From Italy, IO CAPITANO, directed by Matteo Garrone," showing a group of people in a boat; "From Japan, Perfect Days, directed by Wim Wenders," featuring an elderly man tending to plants; and "From Spain, Society of the Snow, directed by J.A. Bayona," depicting a snowy, chaotic environment. The clips are shown with dramatic lighting and emotional intensity, highlighting the diverse themes, with a final visual of a young boy in a school uniform from 'THE BOY AND THE HERON' (Japan), though its audio announcement is not heard in this segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a montage of clips from three international feature film nominees: 'The Teachers’ Lounge' (Germany), 'Io Capitano' (Italy), and 'The Zone of Interest' (United Kingdom), each displaying a distinct visual style and mood. The scene transitions to the Oscars stage, where The Rock, in a shiny silver suit, stands beside a man in a black double-breasted suit with glasses. The man announces, "From Germany, The Teachers' Lounge, directed by Ilker Çatak," and then, "From the United Kingdom, The Zone of Interest, directed by Jonathan Glazer," as the audience applauds. With a slight pause, he declares, "And the Oscar goes to... The Zone of Interest!" The audience erupts in loud cheers and celebratory music as a woman in a black pinstriped sequined suit rises, visibly emotional, and walks down the aisle after hugging a man. The presenter continues, "This is the third nomination for the United Kingdom. The Zone of Interest is the ninth non-English language film to be nominated for both International Feature Film and Best Picture in the same year." The woman then appears on the grand, warm-lit stage with a golden sunburst backdrop, receiving the Oscar from The Rock and the man in the black suit, shaking their hands while the audience continues to cheer enthusiastically as she stands holding her award.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on a grand Oscars stage, bathed in warm, dramatic lighting, featuring a striking red and gold backdrop with radiating golden rods and the words 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE' displayed above. Three men stand on the elevated circular stage. The man on the right, wearing a black suit with a black shirt and clear glasses, stands at a microphone, beginning his speech with a British accent, stating, "Thank you so much. I'm going to read, I'm afraid." As he speaks, a lower-third graphic appears, identifying 'THE ZONE OF INTEREST' as the winner for 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE FILM' from the 'UNITED KINGDOM'. Flanked by two composed men in black tuxedos with bow ties—one of whom, a bald man, steadily holds a golden Oscar statuette—the speaker, appearing emotional and focused in alternating close-ups, thanks the Academy, partners like A24 and Film4, the Polish Film Institute, and the Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum for their trust, as well as his producers, actors, and collaborators. He emphasizes that their film's choices were made "to reflect and confront us in the present," showing "where dehumanization leads at its worst." He continues, "Right now we stand here as men who refute their Jewishness and the Holocaust being hijacked by an occupation which has led to conflict for so many innocent people." This powerful statement elicits a burst of loud, sustained applause from the formally dressed audience, visible in wider shots. Over the continuing applause, he adds, "Whether the victims of October the 7th in Israel, or the ongoing attack on Gaza, all are victims of this dehumanization." He then asks, "How do we resist?" which is met with another wave of strong applause, as the atmosphere remains one of solemn recognition and profound statement.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with a medium shot of three men on a grand Oscars stage, set against a striking red and gold backdrop with radiating golden rods. The man on the right, wearing a black suit with a black shirt and clear glasses, stands at a microphone, reading from a piece of paper. He is flanked by two men in black tuxedos with bow ties; the man on the left holds a golden Oscar statuette. As the man at the microphone speaks, a lower-third graphic appears, identifying the winner as 'THE ZONE OF INTEREST' for 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE FILM' from the 'UNITED KINGDOM'. The camera alternates between close-ups of the speaker, who appears emotional and focused, and wider shots showing the three men standing together. The stage is circular and elevated, with the words 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE' prominently displayed above. The audience, dressed in formal attire, is visible in wide shots, seated in rows and reacting with attention. The lighting is warm and dramatic, emphasizing the golden elements of the stage design. The speaker continues reading, occasionally glancing up, while the two men beside him remain composed, with the man on the left holding the Oscar statuette steadily. The atmosphere is one of solemn recognition and celebration, as the award is formally announced.

The scene transitions to a powerful performance of the Best Song nominee 'WAHZHAZHE (A SONG FOR MY PEOPLE)' by Osage Tribal Singers and Dancers, with music and lyrics by Scott George. A large, prominent white circular drum with a red border, placed on a vibrant red, white, and blue patterned rug, is surrounded by the performers in traditional attire, including hats and beaded necklaces. They strike the drum rhythmically, producing deep, resonant drumming sounds, while singing passionately with powerful vocal harmonies and chanting in a non-English language. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the powerful, rhythmic drumming and communal, passionate singing of the Osage Tribal Singers and Dancers, who are gathered around a large circular drum on a red, white, and blue patterned Performance Rug. Adorned in traditional attire including feathered headdresses, beaded necklaces, and colorful garments, the performers strike the drum rhythmically with sticks, their voices rising in unison in a compelling, non-English chant. The camera alternates between close-ups of individual singers, such as a man in a black hat and patterned tie, and wide-angle shots that reveal the full circular arrangement of the group, emphasizing the collective energy of the performance. The stage is dramatically lit with warm yellow and red hues, further enhanced by a large glowing Full Moon backdrop that contributes to the spiritual atmosphere. Overhead views highlight the tight circle of performers, underscoring the communal nature of the ritual, while the audience remains visible in darkness, captivated by the intense and emotional display. The dynamic lighting, with red vertical lights flanking the stage, creates a powerful visual contrast, all set to the compelling sounds of the performance identified by the 'BEST SONG NOMINEE WAHZHAZHE (A SONG FOR MY PEOPLE)' title card, with music and lyrics by Scott George. The high energy throughout culminates in a unified expression of cultural heritage and identity, driven by both the visual spectacle and the powerful vocal and percussive sounds.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the powerful cultural performance of the Osage Tribal Singers and Dancers, dressed in vibrant traditional attire including feathered headdresses and colorful garments. They rhythmically strike a Large Circular Drum, placed on a red, white, and blue patterned Performance Rug, while delivering powerful, emotional singing. A dramatic, glowing Full Moon against a fiery red and orange sky forms a striking backdrop. The camera pulls back to show the performers silhouetted, as the Oscars Audience, including a Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress and a Man in White Tuxedo, claps loudly and sustains their applause. The scene transitions to the audience, where a Woman in Black-and-White is visibly emotional, wiping tears, while a Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses laughs heartily. The Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo, then walks confidently onto a stage with a cool blue, textured backdrop. He begins his monologue with an engaging, humorous voice, gesturing expressively as he jokes about the show's timing, asking the audience if they're "hanging in" and playfully suggesting they "start the show over again" from the top, eliciting laughter from the audience. He continues with a comparison to an AMC theater, where a movie would just be starting, further engaging the audience with his comedic delivery.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, clapping and speaking to the audience on a grand stage with a blue, textured backdrop, announcing, "Next category is for actor in a supporting role and we have a whole bunch of Oscar winners to support it. Please welcome your best supporting actors!" As he speaks, the camera pulls back to reveal the full Oscars Stage, illuminated with blue lighting and featuring a large golden Oscar statue at center stage, with the words 'SUPPORTING ACTOR' displayed above, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music and applause. Large vertical screens on stage sequentially display images of five nominees for Supporting Actor, each shown holding an Oscar statuette and dressed in formal attire, while the host continues, "And the Oscar goes to... Sam Rockwell, Tim Robbins, Kiefer Sutherland, Christoph Waltz, Mahershala Ali." The nominees include a man with dark hair and a bow tie, a man with blonde hair and a black suit, a man with black hair and glasses, a man with gray hair and glasses, and a man with short black hair and a bow tie. The camera pans across the stage, showing the nominees' images, then cuts to the audience, where the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses is seen smiling, and the Woman in White Dress is seated beside him, both reacting to the presentation amidst the continuing music and applause. The camera then returns to the stage, where the nominees' images are replaced with stills from their nominated performances, showing them in character: one in a black suit with glasses, another in a bowler hat, a third in a patterned sweater, a fourth in a colorful robe, and a fifth in a pinstriped suit with a hat. The audience, including the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses and the Woman in White Dress, watches attentively. The segment concludes with a close-up of the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses, now standing on stage, holding a card and speaking with a focused expression, stating, "It is an honor for us to be here to pay tribute to the nominees for Actor in a Supporting Role. Sterling. I know yours has been a long journey," as the audience applauds.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Black Tuxedo, later identified as Robert Downey Jr., seated in the audience, smiling and looking expectantly toward the stage. The camera then cuts to a split-screen view, showing the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, Ke Huy Quan, on stage holding an envelope labeled 'ACTOR IN A SUPPORTING ROLE' while Robert Downey Jr. watches intently from the audience. Ke Huy Quan speaks animatedly, gesturing with the envelope, stating, "I'm so, so happy that you've finally been recognized by your peers. Uh, your performance in American Fiction is extraordinary and transformative. And I am in awe." Robert Downey Jr.'s expression shifts from calm to surprised as he listens. The stage is grand, with a large golden Oscar statue and sweeping blue panels illuminated with blue lighting, and the words 'SUPPORTING ACTOR' displayed prominently above. The camera briefly cuts to a wide shot of the stage, showing three men standing in front of the Oscar statue, including Ke Huy Quan. The scene returns to the split-screen, where Ke Huy Quan continues, "The magic you bring on screen, it's outmatched only by the magic of who you are as a person," and Robert Downey Jr.’s eyes widen further in surprise. The audience erupts in applause and cheers, and the Woman in White Dress beside Robert Downey Jr. claps enthusiastically. The camera then focuses on Robert Downey Jr., who stands up, looking stunned, as the audience continues to cheer. The scene shifts to a close-up of Ke Huy Quan, now standing alone on stage, looking serious and composed. The camera then cuts to the audience, where Ke Huy Quan is now seated, clapping along with others, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, the Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, the Man in White Tuxedo, and the Woman in Black-and-White. Ke Huy Quan's voice continues, joking, "There are actors. And then there are actors who don't drop character until the DVD commentary. I'm talking about Robert Downey Jr." The audience responds with laughter and applause as Ke Huy Quan adds, "That dude. Susan's husband." The atmosphere is celebratory and electric, with the audience fully engaged in the moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses, seated in the audience, smiling and reacting to the ceremony. The camera then cuts to a split-screen view showing him on the right and the Man in Dark Suit with Glasses on the left, who is gesturing animatedly as he speaks, praising an actor's versatility: "He could be Iron Man, he could be every man. He always goes above and beyond... as Chaplin, he learned how to play the violin left-handed. Who does that, seriously? It's obnoxious. And now in Oppenheimer, he steals scenes from Albert Einstein and the atomic bomb. I love him, you love him. We couldn't be happier for his third Oscar nom for his amazing portrayal as Lewis Strauss," eliciting laughter and applause. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the Oscars Stage, illuminated with blue lighting and featuring a large golden Oscar statue at center stage, with the words 'SUPPORTING ACTOR' displayed above. The camera then focuses on the Oscars Host, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing on stage with a blue, textured backdrop, clapping and speaking to the audience. He continues the introductions, stating, "In the course of his brilliant career, Robert De Niro has teamed... has teamed with Martin Scorsese to bring us a wide range of uniquely complicated villains. His latest performance in Killers of the Flower Moon delivers a different type of evil, not of a mobster or psychotic loner, but of a businessman consumed..." as the audience responds with applause and cheers. The scene shifts back to the audience, showing the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses clapping, alongside the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, who is also clapping and smiling. The camera then returns to the Oscars Host, who continues to speak, followed by a shot of the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses and the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress in the audience, both appearing engaged and attentive. The segment concludes with a close-up of the Oscars Host, who is still speaking, and a final shot of the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses and the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, both clapping and reacting to the ceremony.
