segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen view, showing Robert De Niro in a black tuxedo and glasses in the audience, smiling subtly and nodding, while on the right, Tim Robbins, also in a black tuxedo, stands on a stage with a deep blue curtain, speaking animatedly and gesturing. Robbins declares that someone was "consumed by greed and his lust for power," then addresses "Mr. De Niro," praising his "Oscar-worthy performance" that "should be winning," eliciting audience laughter and applause. He continues, stating De Niro "gives us a reminder of the heart of darkness within our ancestral blood," concluding with "Congratulations to an acting legend," as the audience erupts in cheers and applause, accompanied by swelling celebratory music. The scene cuts to a wider shot of the Oscars stage with three men in tuxedos clapping in front of a large golden Oscar statue, then focuses on the gray-haired Oscars Host in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, who speaks with a warm, engaging expression. The camera shifts to the audience, where Ryan Gosling, in a black suit, watches intently, briefly touching his mouth thoughtfully, seated next to a woman in a black-and-gold sequined dress. The video returns to a split-screen, with Gosling on the left smiling and reacting, while the Oscars Host on the right continues his speech with expressive hand movements, remarking that "certain male behavior looks like the inferiority complex of a plastic doll in a cardboard box," but it's "something easy to ridicule." He then asks, "So why then do we admire Ryan Gosling's Ken so much?" before admitting, "Because Ryan's portrayal of Ken allows us to... connect," as the audio fades, maintaining the celebratory and polished atmosphere typical of the Oscars ceremony.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a split-screen view: on the left, Robert De Niro sits in the audience, wearing a black tuxedo with a black tie and glasses, smiling subtly and nodding as he watches the stage. On the right, Tim Robbins stands on stage in a black tuxedo with a black tie, speaking animatedly and gesturing with his right hand, his expression shifting from serious to amused. The background behind Robbins is a deep blue curtain with diagonal light streaks. The scene then cuts to a wider shot of the stage, where three men in tuxedos stand in front of a large golden Oscar statue, clapping. The camera focuses on one of them, the Oscars Host, who is wearing a black tuxedo with a bow tie and has gray hair; he speaks with a warm, engaging expression. The scene shifts back to the audience, where Ryan Gosling, seated next to a woman in a black-and-gold sequined dress, watches intently, his hand briefly touching his mouth in a thoughtful gesture. The video returns to a split-screen, showing Gosling on the left, smiling and reacting, and the Oscars Host on the right, continuing his speech with expressive hand movements. The lighting remains consistent throughout, with stage lights highlighting the speakers and audience members in formal attire, creating a celebratory and polished atmosphere typical of the Oscars ceremony.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen view, featuring Ryan Gosling in a black suit with a silver necklace, subtly smiling and thoughtfully touching his mouth on the left, while on the right, the Oscars Host, in a classic black tuxedo, speaks animatedly on stage against a deep blue backdrop. The host's voice is heard praising a film as "funny, intelligent, hugely entertaining, and ultimately poignant and deserving of empathy," concluding that "to experience all that is exactly what we go to the movies for." This statement is met with a burst of enthusiastic audience applause and cheers. The scene transitions to a close-up of Ryan Gosling clapping warmly, seated beside a Woman in Black-and-Gold Sequined Dress who also applauds. The camera then cuts to the stage, where the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses, wearing a black tuxedo, white shirt, tinted glasses, and a red boutonniere, continues speaking with enthusiasm, announcing "Mark Ruffalo" to another wave of loud applause and cheers from the audience. He proceeds to commend Ruffalo's "over three decades" of work, highlighting his "versatility from drama, to comedy, to action," and his "unmatched appetite for challenges," exemplified by his portrayal of Duncan Weatherbird in "Poor Things," which "rightfully earned" him this nomination. The audience is shown reacting, including the Man in Black Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere, with curly gray hair and a beard, seated beside a smiling Woman in Black-and-White and a Man in Black Tuxedo. The split-screen returns, showing the Man in Black Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere on the left and the Man in Tuxedo with Glasses on the right, both engaged, with the latter pointing and speaking with energy, as the audience continues to applaud and cheer. The atmosphere remains formal and celebratory, with focused lighting emphasizing the grandeur of the Oscars event.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Black Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere, smiling and clapping alongside Sterling K. Brown, both in black tuxedos with red boutonnieres, as a male voice, identified as the Man in Dark Suit with Glasses, praises 'Mark' (Ruffalo), stating, 'And beyond your talent, Mark, you're simply a wonderful human being. Thank you,' which is met with applause from the Oscars Audience. The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the Oscars Stage, displaying 'SUPPORTING ACTOR' above a golden Oscar statue, where the Man in Dark Suit with Glasses, in a maroon tuxedo, stands at the podium holding an envelope. He builds suspense, announcing, 'And the Oscar goes to...' before loudly proclaiming, 'Robert Downey Jr.!' This triggers an eruption of cheers, applause, and celebratory music. A graphic appears listing the five nominees, including Robert Downey Jr., who reacts with surprise, covering his mouth, then quickly stands and rushes down the aisle through the applauding Oscars Audience. As he runs energetically across the stage, waving to the crowd, the Man in Dark Suit with Glasses provides context, noting, 'This is the first Oscar win for Robert Downey Jr. and his third nomination.' Robert Downey Jr. is greeted by the announcer, who hands him the Oscar. He then approaches the podium, where a digital nameplate reads 'ROBERT DOWNEY JR. ACTOR IN A SUPPORTING ROLE OPPENHEIMER'. Holding the Oscar, he begins his acceptance speech with a composed and emotional expression, his black tuxedo with a black shirt visible against the blue backdrop, immediately saying, 'Thank you, thank you,' and humorously adding, 'Uh, thanks Sam, thanks fellas, that's amazing, you only flubbed one line,' which elicits laughter from the audience.

segments.integrated_caption: Robert Downey Jr., impeccably dressed in a sleek black tuxedo with a black shirt, clear-rimmed glasses, and a silver crescent-shaped lapel pin, stands on the grand Oscars stage framed by deep blue velvet curtains and warm golden lighting. He begins his acceptance speech for Best Supporting Actor with a warm, reflective expression, humorously thanking his "terrible childhood and the Academy in that order." As he picks up the golden Oscar statuette, holding it close, he continues, correcting himself from "veterinarian" to "wife," and points out "Susan Downey over there," prompting enthusiastic applause and cheers from the formally dressed audience. He expresses deep gratitude, stating she "found me a snarling rescue pet and you loved me back to life," which he credits for his presence. The audience, including a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress (Susan Downey) and a man in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, claps and smiles attentively. Downey Jr. then shares his "little secret," admitting he "needed this job more than it needed me," acknowledging "Chris," "Emma," and the "great casts and crews of all time" including "Emily, Killian, Matt Damon, Blunt," for making him a "better man." He emphasizes the meaningfulness of their work before humorously returning to thank his publicist, agent "Philip Raskin," and "Andrew Dun," conveying a sense of deep appreciation and emotion throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Robert Downey Jr. on stage at the Oscars, wearing a black tuxedo, clear-rimmed glasses, and a silver crescent-shaped lapel pin, holding his Oscar statuette. He delivers an emotional and humorous acceptance speech, thanking his "Dunmother Joey Feely" and his stylist Erica, eliciting laughter and applause from the audience. He then humorously recounts how his entertainment lawyer, Tom Hansen, spent 40 years trying to get him insured and "bailing me out of the hoosegow," before dedicating the award to "Avery X and India." As he speaks, gesturing expressively and lifting the Oscar high, the camera pulls back to reveal the grand circular stage with blue lighting and a large golden Oscar statue, with formally dressed individuals like Tim Robbins and Mark Ruffalo standing behind him. The scene cuts to the audience, where notable figures such as Martin Scorsese, Jodie Foster, and a black-and-white dog wearing a bow tie are seen clapping and smiling, along with other attendees like a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress and a woman in a black sequined blazer. The audience's enthusiastic applause swells as the lighting dramatically shifts to vibrant purple and pink hues. An upbeat, celebratory synth-pop track begins to play, accompanying a performer in a sparkling pink jacket and black hat who takes the stage, holding a microphone and engaging the crowd with animated gestures, while a quick shot shows a woman in a black-and-white checkered jacket on a pink background, speaking into a microphone, as the atmosphere transitions from a formal ceremony to a lively, musical celebration.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Ryan Gosling, dazzling in a fully sequined pink suit and dark sunglasses, performing with intense energy on stage at what sounds like a lively awards ceremony. He sings the emotional lyrics, "doesn't seem to matter what I do, I'm always number two," into a microphone, his powerful male vocal carrying over a building pop-rock ballad. The cheering audience is bathed in vibrant purple and pink lighting, with a woman in a sparkly strapless dress seen laughing and covering her mouth, clearly amused. A lower-third graphic identifies the song as 'I'M JUST KEN,' a Best Song nominee performed by Ryan Gosling with music and lyrics by Mark Ronson and Andrew Wyatt. As Gosling continues to sing about having "feelings I can't explain, driving me insane," he walks forward, joined by a bassist in a white shirt playing alongside him. The stage then dramatically transitions to a set of pink tiered steps, where Gosling, now singing the chorus "I'm just Ken, anywhere else I'd be a ten," is joined by a group of male dancers dressed in black suits, white shirts, and black cowboy hats. They perform a synchronized, theatrical dance routine, clapping, tipping their hats, and gesturing dramatically as Gosling sings the poignant lines, "Is it my destiny to live and die a life of blonde fragility? I'm just Ken, where I see love, she sees a friend. What will it take for her to see the man behind the tan and fight for me?" at the center. The atmosphere is electric, theatrical, and highly stylized, with the entire stage bathed in pink and purple hues, creating a surreal, almost dreamlike performance space, all underscored by the driving music and continued audience applause.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with Ryan Gosling, dazzling in a fully sequined pink suit and dark sunglasses, commanding a vibrant circular stage bathed in intense pink and purple lighting. He passionately sings the lead vocals to the upbeat pop-rock song "I'm Just Ken," his voice conveying the character's longing with lyrics like "I'm just Ken, anywhere else I'd be a ten. Is it my destiny to live and die a life of blonde fragility?" A guitarist in a black leather jacket and top hat plays a sunburst electric guitar alongside him, while male dancers in black suits and cowboy hats perform synchronized choreography on tiered pink steps, clapping and gesturing dramatically. The stage backdrop features a large, glowing red circular screen displaying 'Ken' in bold white letters, reinforcing the song's theme. As Gosling continues to sing, "What will it take for her to see the man behind the tan and fight for me? I'm just Ken, and I'm enough," he moves through the packed audience, interacting with guests, including a woman in a sparkling silver dress who laughs and dances with him. On-screen text like 'Check Me Out' and 'I'm Just Ken' flashes, directly complementing his sung lines, "So hey, check me out, yeah, I'm just Ken. My name's Ken, so am I." The grand, futuristic venue, with its curved arches and star-like ceiling lights, is filled with the sound of the music and an enthusiastic, cheering audience, as Gosling returns to center stage, surrounded by his dancers and musicians, with lights pulsing to the rhythm.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the grand Oscars stage, bathed in warm golden lighting, featuring a large circular screen at its center displaying a montage of film scenes. Elegant, curved golden arches flank the stage, while the audience, seated in darkness, focuses their attention forward, their applause and cheers filling the air. Da’Vine Joy Randolph, striking in an emerald green gown with dramatic ruffled shoulders and sparkling earrings, and Ariana Grande, in a voluminous, ruffled pink strapless gown with her blonde hair in an elegant updo, walk onto the stage. They approach the central podium, where Da’Vine Joy Randolph takes the microphone, her clear voice announcing, "We're here to present the Oscar for Original Song and Original Score. A great movie song can set the mood, reveal something about a character, and help us tell a story." Ariana Grande stands smiling beside her, occasionally glancing at her, then adds, "And a great film score can even leave a handprint on our hearts forever. It can ignite wonder and astonishment, make us feel sadness and longing, and can even transport us to new worlds." Da’Vine Joy Randolph continues, "Or somewhere over the rainbow where the dreams that you dare to dream really do come true." Ariana Grande playfully responds, "Oh, how wickedly subtle of you," to which Da’Vine Joy Randolph replies, "I do try." As Da’Vine Joy Randolph speaks, the circular screen behind them transitions to a split-screen montage of scenes from 'American Fiction,' including a woman in a white blouse and large sunglasses, a man in a white shirt walking by the ocean, and a woman in a white dress. The on-screen text 'AMERICAN FICTION' and 'LAURA KARPMAN' appears at the bottom. Da’Vine Joy Randolph then announces, "And now, here are the nominees for Original Score," followed by a voiceover stating, "American Fiction by Laura Karpman," as the visual tribute continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a cinematic montage of Best Original Score nominees, accompanied by their respective scores. A stylized piano-key visual effect introduces 'INDIANA JONES AND THE DIAL OF DESTINY' by John Williams, as a female voice announces the film and composer, showing an older man examining a golden artifact and a vintage car. This transitions to 'KILLERS OF THE FLOWER MOON' by Robbie Robertson, also announced by the female voice, depicting a man in a suit in a 1920s town and a dimly lit saloon. Next, 'OPPENHEIMER' by Ludwig Göransson is presented, with the female voice announcing it, showing a man in a checkered jacket in a car and a serious man in a suit. Finally, 'POOR THINGS' by Jerskin Fendrix is introduced by the female voice, featuring a woman in a yellow dress in an ornate interior and a man holding a glass. The montage concludes with a grand reveal of the 'ORIGINAL SCORE' category on a golden, circular stage. Da’Vine Joy Randolph, in a striking emerald green gown, and Ariana Grande, in a voluminous pink strapless gown, stand at the podium. Da’Vine Joy Randolph announces, "And the Oscar goes to..." before Ariana Grande reveals, "Oppenheimer. Ludwig Göransson." The audience erupts in applause and cheers as Ludwig Göransson, dressed in a black suit, rises from his seat, smiling and clapping. He walks down the aisle through the cheering crowd to the stage, where he is greeted by the presenters and joyfully receives the Oscar statuette. A male announcer's voice highlights, "This is a second Oscar win and third nomination for Ludwig Göransson, who took home his first Oscar in this category for Black Panther," as the audience continues to applaud.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a cinematic montage of nominated films for Best Original Score, each displayed through a stylized piano-key visual effect. The first clip features 'AMERICAN FICTION' with Laura Karpmans score, showing a man in a white shirt and another in a blue jacket shaking hands in a sunlit room. This transitions to 'INDIANA JONES AND THE DIAL OF DESTINY' with John Williams' score, depicting an older man in glasses and a hat examining a golden artifact, followed by a vintage car driving through a dusty street. Next, 'KILLERS OF THE FLOWER MOON' with Robbie Robertson's score is shown, featuring a man in a suit and hat walking through a 1920s town, then sitting in a dimly lit saloon. The montage continues with 'OPPENHEIMER' by Ludwig Göransson, showing a man in a checkered jacket in a car, then a close-up of a man in a suit with a serious expression. Finally, 'POOR THINGS' by Jerskin Fendrix is displayed, with a woman in a yellow dress walking through an ornate, warmly lit interior, followed by a man in a dark shirt holding a glass. The montage concludes with a grand reveal of the 'ORIGINAL SCORE' category on a golden, circular stage. Two presenters, Da’Vine Joy Randolph in a striking emerald green gown with dramatic ruffled shoulders and Ariana Grande in a voluminous pink strapless gown with her blonde hair in an elegant updo, stand at the podium. They open the envelope and announce the winner. The audience erupts in applause as Ludwig Göransson, dressed in a black suit, rises from his seat, smiling and clapping, and walks down the aisle through the cheering crowd. He reaches the stage, where he is greeted by the presenters, and receives the Oscar statuette with a joyful expression, as the audience continues to applaud.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens to the sound of loud applause and cheering as Ludwig Göransson, dressed in a sleek black tuxedo, walks onto the Oscars stage holding his golden Oscar statuette. He approaches the podium with a joyful, slightly overwhelmed expression, smiling broadly, and exclaims "Wow" and "Um" before beginning his acceptance speech. He thanks Christopher Nolan and Emma Thomas for inviting him on this "incredible world," crediting Nolan for the idea to use a violin in the score, which allowed him to collaborate with his "wonderful wife and acclaimed violinist, Serena Göransson." The audience responds with another round of applause. Göransson continues, sharing a personal anecdote about recording at night and rushing home to put their kids, Apollo and Romeo, down to bed, noting that the result set a "really nice tone for the film." As he speaks, the camera cuts to the audience, showing a man in a black tuxedo with glasses clapping and a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress smiling. The camera returns to Göransson, who uses expressive hand gestures, occasionally glancing at the audience. A lower-third graphic identifies him as 'LUDWIG GÖRANSSON, ORIGINAL SCORE, OPPENHEIMER.' He then thanks his music team—Teresa Stanislav, Jake Brown, Chris Fogel, Anthony Parther, and Alyssa Park—for making the music sound great, and emotionally thanks his parents for giving him "guitars and drum machines instead of video games." He concludes with a heartfelt "Thank you!" as the camera pulls back to a wide shot, revealing Da’Vine Joy Randolph in a striking emerald green gown and Ariana Grande in a voluminous, ruffled pink strapless gown standing beside him on stage. The audience is shown clapping and cheering loudly, accompanied by celebratory orchestral music, as Göransson finishes his speech, smiling and nodding appreciatively before stepping back from the podium, the golden stage lighting enhancing the grandeur of the moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with celebratory orchestral music as Da’Vine Joy Randolph, in an emerald green gown, and Ariana Grande, in a voluminous pink gown, stand at the Oscars podium. Da’Vine Joy Randolph, speaking with a clear, resonant voice, introduces the award, stating, "All night you've seen performances of this year's nominated songs. And now, it's time for the award." Ariana Grande then announces, with a clear, excited voice, "And the Oscar goes to... Billie Eilish and Finneas O’Connell!" The scene cuts to the audience, where Billie Eilish, in a black blazer and checkered skirt, and Finneas O’Connell, in a black tuxedo, react with surprise and joy as their names are called for 'What Was I Made For?' from 'Barbie'. The Oscars Audience erupts in enthusiastic cheers and applause. An off-screen male announcer confirms, "This is the second Oscar win and second nomination for Billie Eilish and Finneas O’Connell. The siblings took home their first Oscar for the song 'No Time to Die'." Billie and Finneas rise, hugging and celebrating with friends and colleagues, including a woman in a black sequined blazer and a man in a black tuxedo, as the celebratory music swells. They make their way down the aisle, embracing others, to continued applause. Upon reaching the stage, they are greeted with hugs from Da’Vine Joy Randolph and Ariana Grande. Billie and Finneas stand at the podium, holding their Oscars, with Billie speaking into the microphone, visibly moved. A lower-third graphic appears, reading: 'WHAT WAS I MADE FOR? BILLIE EILISH, FINNEAS O’CONNELL ORIGINAL SONG BARBIE'. The atmosphere is celebratory and emotional, with warm golden lighting illuminating the stage and the audience’s enthusiastic reactions.

segments.integrated_caption: Billie Eilish, dressed in a black blazer over a white ruffled blouse with a red pin on her lapel, stands emotionally at the Oscars podium, holding her statuette. With a tearful, trembling voice, she confesses, "I had a nightmare about this last night," before expressing profound gratitude to the Academy, stating, "I just didn't think this would happen. I was not expecting this. I feel so incredibly lucky and honored." She then directly addresses Greta Gerwig, who is seen clapping and smiling warmly in the audience alongside Margot Robbie, saying, "Thank you to Greta, where did you go? I love you! Thank you for this!" Billie continues, "I'm so grateful for this song and for this movie and the way that it made me feel," dedicating the award to "everyone who was affected by the movie and how incredible it is," and thanking her team and parents with an emotional "I love you guys so much." Finneas O’Connell, in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, stands supportively beside her, then takes the microphone. With a heartfelt voice, he thanks Paul Walter, Inon Kreyts, Mark, and Andrew for their work on the song, and specifically mentions "Margo, of course, Lucky Chap, Tom," before concluding with a heartfelt "Thank you to our parents forever and ever and ever." The siblings exchange smiles and a light laugh, clearly moved by their shared triumph as the on-screen graphic displays 'What Was I Made For?' by Billie Eilish & Finneas O’Connell from Barbie, all bathed in warm golden light with elegant curved arches in the background.
segments.visual_original_caption: Billie Eilish and Finneas O’Connell stand at the Oscars podium, having just won the award for Best Original Song for 'What Was I Made For?' from the film Barbie. Billie, dressed in a black blazer over a white ruffled blouse with a red pin on her lapel, holds the Oscar statuette and speaks emotionally into the microphone, her expression shifting between gratitude and tears. Finneas, in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, stands beside her, listening intently and occasionally glancing at her with a supportive smile. The stage is bathed in warm golden light with elegant curved arches in the background. As Billie speaks, the camera cuts to the audience, showing Greta Gerwig and Margot Robbie clapping and smiling warmly. Billie then turns to Finneas, who takes the microphone and delivers a brief, heartfelt speech, gesturing with his hands and sharing a moment of joy with her. The pair exchange smiles and a light laugh, clearly moved by the moment. The on-screen graphic at the bottom of the screen displays the title of the winning song, the artists' names, and the film it’s from. The atmosphere is celebratory and intimate, capturing a genuine moment of shared triumph between the siblings.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Billie Eilish, visibly emotional with tears and red lipstick, holding an Oscar statuette and speaking into a microphone on a grand stage bathed in warm golden lighting with elegant, curved golden arches. Dressed in a black tuxedo with a red pin, she thanks her best friend Zoe for playing Barbies, her dance teachers, and choir teachers Miss Brigham (for believing in her) and Miss T (for being good at her job despite not liking her), concluding with a heartfelt 'Thank you to the Academy. I love you.' Beside her, Finneas O’Connell, in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere, curly gray hair, and a silver brooch, holds his Oscar, smiling and gesturing as he thanks his best friends David and Rach, Claudia, and later Joe Marianelli. The audience, including a man in a black tuxedo with a red boutonniere and a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress, claps and cheers enthusiastically. The scene transitions to the Oscars Host, a man in a beige patterned tuxedo with a black bow tie, gesturing on a stage with a blue textured backdrop. He announces there are only four awards left, then introduces the 'Best Actor' category, welcoming all five original members of NSYNC to present. The camera pulls back to reveal the stage now bathed in purple lighting, with the words 'LEAD ACTOR' illuminated above a large golden Oscar statue, flanked by flowing purple drapes and floral arrangements, as the engaged audience watches. The video concludes with a close-up of the golden Oscar statue, the focal point on the elaborately decorated stage.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the grand Oscars stage, featuring a circular design with flowing purple drapes and floral arrangements, bathed in soft, dramatic lighting. Five large vertical screens initially display the 'LEAD ACTOR' nominees, each holding an Oscar statuette: Nicolas Cage, Matthew McConaughey, Brendan Fraser, Paul Giamatti, and a humorous, fictional Barack Obama. Above, the 'LEAD ACTOR' sign glows in golden letters. As the camera pans, the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses and the Man in Dark Suit walk onto the stage from behind the screens, while the formally dressed audience claps and reacts with excitement. A voice is heard listing a different set of nominees: 'Nicolas Cage, Matthew McConaughey, Brendan Fraser, Ben Kingsley, Forest Whitaker,' followed by a swell of background music and sustained audience applause and cheering. The presenters stand before the screens, which then transition to show new nominees: Paul Giamatti, Harrison Ford, Cord Jefferson, Cillian Murphy, and Samuel L. Jackson. The Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses, speaking with an engaging tone, addresses the audience, stating, 'We're here to speak about the great talent of this year's five nominees for actor in a leading role.' He continues, 'When you see Jeffrey Wright in a film,' prompting loud cheers from the audience, 'You instantly know it's going to be good because he's so good.' The camera cuts to Cord Jefferson in the audience, smiling and clapping with a woman in a sparkly dress beside him. The Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses earnestly praises Jeffrey Wright, saying, 'Jeffrey, in American Fiction, you are funny and furious and flawed and empathetic, and for the way that you elevate the craft, we're indebted to you,' as the audience responds with more cheers and applause. The stage remains vibrant with purple and gold lighting, the large screens dynamically showcasing the nominees, maintaining a celebratory and theatrical atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the Oscars stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue centered against a backdrop of flowing purple drapes and floral arrangements, accompanied by enthusiastic applause and cheering. Four men in tuxedos stand on stage, with the focus shifting to Nicolas Cage, who speaks animatedly, his expression intense and expressive, discussing how actors go "above and beyond" for roles, citing Daniel Day-Lewis and Paul Giamatti's commitment, before exclaiming, "But the point is, you did do it, Paul! And you were brilliant again. Bravo!" The camera cuts to the audience, showing a bald man with a gray beard in a black tuxedo (the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses) seated between two women, smiling and reacting to Cage’s monologue with laughter. A split-screen effect then juxtaposes Cage on stage with the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses in the audience, who laughs heartily as Cage gestures emphatically. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the stage with the illuminated sign 'LEAD ACTOR' above, as the presenters continue amidst continued applause. The focus shifts to a man in a brown tuxedo with a black bow tie and a red boutonniere (the Man in Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere) who speaks with a warm, engaging demeanor, introducing "a modern-day Renaissance man," Mr. Bradley Cooper, who "wrote, directed, produced, and acted in his latest film, Maestro." The camera cuts to the audience, where Adam Sandler, seated next to a woman in sparkly sunglasses (the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses), smiles and claps, clearly amused, while the woman next to him laughs heartily. A split-screen again shows the Man in Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere on stage and Adam Sandler in the audience, both sharing a moment of mutual amusement. The atmosphere is lighthearted and celebratory, with the audience reacting with laughter and applause to the host’s jokes and the presenters’ interactions.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a split-screen view, showing Matthew McConaughey on the left, dressed in a brown tuxedo with a black bow tie and a red boutonniere, speaking with expressive gestures as his voice is heard praising a performance, stating, "Bradley takes a transformative journey into becoming Leonard Bernstein, bring to life all the character charisma and complex brilliance of an American icon. Congratulations." On the right, Adam Sandler, in a black tuxedo with an open collar, smiles warmly in the audience next to a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress with large sparkly sunglasses, both reacting to the ongoing presentation. Following applause, the scene transitions to a wide shot of the grand Oscars stage, where a large golden Oscar statue is centered against a backdrop of flowing purple drapes and floral arrangements, with three presenters standing. The camera then focuses on Ben Kingsley, a bald man in a black double-breasted tuxedo with a black bow tie and a sparkling silver flower-shaped lapel pin, standing center stage with a serious, composed demeanor as his voice clearly announces, "Cillian Murphy," which is met with immediate, loud cheers and applause from the Oscars audience. The video cuts to Cillian Murphy, seated in a black tuxedo with a white pleated shirt and a gold circular lapel pin, reacting with a mix of surprise and emotion, his eyes wide and mouth slightly open upon hearing his name. The split-screen returns, juxtaposing Ben Kingsley’s stoic expression with Cillian Murphy’s animated reaction, while Ben Kingsley's voice continues, describing how Cillian Murphy "accepted the leading role of Oppenheimer before even reading the script," and praising the "masterful" performance, noting his portrayal with "layers of humanity" whilst his character created "something inhuman," calling it "riveting to watch." The audience is shown clapping, with several guests visible, including a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress with a choker and another in a black sequined dress, all engaged in the moment. The warm, theatrical lighting emphasizes the grandeur of the Oscars ceremony, as the camera alternates between close-ups of the stage and the audience to capture the emotional weight of the announcement and subsequent praise.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the Oscars stage, bathed in golden lighting, featuring a large golden Oscar statue centered against flowing purple drapes and floral arrangements. Four men in tuxedos stand on stage, as upbeat, celebratory orchestral music plays and the audience applauds loudly. The focus shifts to Jeffrey Wright, a bald man with glasses and a beard, wearing a black patterned tuxedo, who speaks expressively with hand gestures, addressing Colman Domingo directly: "Colman. When I first worked with you... a decade or two ago. I saw that you were bold, brilliant, bound for greatness. It's been exciting to watch you light up the screen over the years, most recently with your truly inspired portrayal of civil rights leader Bayard Rustin." The camera cuts to the audience, highlighting Colman Domingo, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and a gold brooch, who is seated and adjusts his bow tie with a subtle smile, while others around him watch attentively. Jeffrey Wright continues, "Colman, your genius as an actor illuminates the genius of an angelic troublemaker who's nearly buried in the history books. Thankfully, Justin is getting his due, as are you. Colman Domingo." A wave of enthusiastic audience applause and cheers erupts, accompanied by swelling orchestral music, as a split-screen then shows Jeffrey Wright on stage and Colman Domingo in the audience, emphasizing their connection. The scene transitions to a wide view of the stage with the illuminated sign 'LEAD ACTOR' above, as the audience is shown in full. The camera then focuses on a presenter in a black tuxedo holding a card, standing beside a graphic displaying the five nominees for 'Actor in a Leading Role': Jeffrey Wright, Paul Giamatti, Bradley Cooper, Cillian Murphy, and Colman Domingo, each shown in circular frames with their names below. The nominees are shown reacting in the audience, with Cillian Murphy, a man in a black tuxedo, visibly surprised and animated, while others smile or nod. The presenter's voice is heard saying, "And the Oscar goes to..." as the formal and celebratory atmosphere, with its elegant stage design, enhances the prestige of the moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Presenter in Black Tuxedo declaring "Cillian Murphy!" as the winner for Best Actor in a Leading Role for 'Oppenheimer', immediately triggering a wave of loud, enthusiastic applause and cheers from the Oscars Audience. Cillian Murphy, dressed in a classic black tuxedo with a white pleated shirt and a gold circular lapel pin, reacts with wide-eyed surprise and an open-mouthed expression of disbelief from his seat. As upbeat, celebratory orchestral music begins to play, he rises and is warmly embraced by Greta Gerwig, who is in a sparkling silver gown, smiling and clapping joyfully. He then moves through the standing, applauding audience, hugging Robert Downey Jr., who beams with pride. The Presenter's voiceover notes, "This is the first Oscar win and nomination for Cillian Murphy," as Murphy continues his walk toward the Oscars Stage. He is greeted by the Presenter in Black Tuxedo, who hands him the Oscar statuette. Murphy then walks onto the circular stage, illuminated with purple drapes and a large golden Oscar statue in the background, with a lower-third graphic identifying him as 'CILLIAN MURPHY, ACTOR IN A LEADING ROLE, OPPENHEIMER'. Approaching the podium, he is visibly emotional, placing a hand on his face, and after a soft "Oh" and a slightly overwhelmed "Um, I'm a little overwhelmed," he begins his acceptance speech, gesturing expressively and thanking "the Academy," "Chris Nolan and Emma Thomas," before stating, "It's been the wildest, most..." The audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress with Sunglasses and the Man in White Tuxedo, continues to applaud in the celebratory atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Cillian Murphy, impeccably dressed in a black tuxedo with a gold lapel pin, delivering an emotional acceptance speech for Lead Actor at the Oscars podium. He expresses profound gratitude to the Oppenheimer cast and crew for an "exhilarating" and "creatively satisfying journey," and acknowledges his fellow nominees with awe. As Murphy speaks, gesturing expressively, the audience, including a smiling Man in Tuxedo with Glasses and a Woman in Black Ruffled Gown, claps in appreciation. He thanks his "incredible team," specifically Craig Bankey, and lovingly names his family: Brendan Murphy, Mary Murphy, Yvonne McGuinness (his partner in life and art), and his sons Malachy and Aran, prompting cheers and applause from the crowd. Murphy proudly declares, "I'm a very proud Irishman standing here tonight," as the camera cuts to a wide shot revealing the grand stage with the illuminated 'LEAD ACTOR' sign. He is joined by presenters and nominees, such as a Man in Black Tuxedo with Red Boutonniere and a Woman in Silver Beaded Gown, all applauding. The audience, including a joyful Bald Man in Black Tuxedo and a Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, claps enthusiastically. Murphy concludes by dedicating his award to "the peacemakers everywhere," referencing the film's subject, as the crowd erupts in cheers and applause, and celebratory music swells, with Murphy raising his arm in triumph under warm, golden lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a smiling man in a black suit and white shirt, clapping alongside a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress with large sparkly sunglasses, who is also clapping and laughing, as triumphant orchestral music begins to play. The camera then pulls back to reveal a wide shot of the grand Oscars stage, illuminated with purple drapes, a large golden Oscar statue, and the word 'OSCARS' prominently displayed above, while the audience stands and applauds. Amidst swelling music and cheers, Steven Spielberg, a man with white hair and glasses, wearing a black tuxedo with a bow tie, is introduced as the Academy Award-winning director of Schindler's List, walking onto the stage from the audience. He turns to face the crowd as he approaches the circular podium, holding a beige envelope labeled 'DIRECTING', and begins to speak into the microphone with a composed and respectful demeanor, stating that "At their very best, films can do more than entertain us. They can open our hearts to each other. They can show us ourselves, and they can shine a light on where we've been and make us think about where we are going." He continues, "We are so fortunate that this has been a great and eclectic year for movies, and a tribute to all the places these five nominated directors have taken the entire world." A dramatic musical sting then plays as Spielberg announces "Justine Triet, Anatomy of a Fall," coinciding with a film clip of a woman in a beige coat and red scarf holding a cup, followed by applause. He then announces "Martin Scorsese," as a clip of an older man in a suit and hat walking through a dusty, vintage town with children appears, again met with applause. The ceremony's dramatic lighting, with bright spotlights on the stage and a darkened audience, maintains a formal and celebratory atmosphere throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a visual montage of behind-the-scenes footage, showing Christopher Nolan on the *Oppenheimer* set with an IMAX camera, Yorgos Lanthimos directing *Poor Things*, and Jonathan Glazer filming *The Zone of Interest*. The scene then shifts to the Oscars stage, where the Man in Black Tuxedo with Glasses, the presenter, stands before a large golden Oscar statue and purple drapes. He announces the nominees for Best Director, his clear voice naming "killers of the flower moon," "Christopher Nolan, Oppenheimer," "Yorgos Lanthimos, Poor Things," and "Jonathan Glazer, The Zone of Interest," as their names and film clips are displayed on a circular graphic. The presenter then declares, "And the Oscar goes to... Christopher Nolan, Oppenheimer!" The Oscars Audience erupts in enthusiastic applause and cheers as Christopher Nolan rises from his seat. A distinct voice-over announces, "This is his first Oscar win, an eighth nomination for Christopher Nolan. He received his first nomination for his momento screenplay." Nolan walks down the aisle, embracing colleagues like Robert Downey Jr. and Cillian Murphy, and is warmly greeted on stage by the presenter with a hug and a kiss. Visibly moved, Nolan accepts the Oscar statuette, holding it with both hands, and approaches the podium, where he is heard saying "Thank you" before beginning his acceptance speech with "Uh, there," as the on-screen text confirms 'CHRISTOPHER NOLAN, DIRECTING, OPPENHEIMER.' The stage is bathed in warm golden light, reflecting the celebratory atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: Christopher Nolan, dressed in a classic black tuxedo with a bow tie, stands at the Oscars podium holding his golden Oscar statuette for Best Director for *Oppenheimer*. The stage, adorned with deep purple drapes and a large golden Oscar silhouette, creates a formal and celebratory atmosphere. Nolan speaks with emotion, occasionally gesturing with his free hand while holding the award securely, appearing reflective and grateful. He begins by thanking "so many people who've dragged me up here," specifically mentioning Universal Studios and Donna Langley for "seeing the potential." He then thanks Kai Bird and Martin Sherwin for their 25 years on the book the film is based on. As he continues, the camera alternates between medium shots and close-ups, capturing his expressions. He expresses gratitude to "the most incredible cast," naming Matt Damon, Robert, Emily, and Florence, and highlighting "the incredible Cillian Murphy" as a leader. He also thanks the crew, some of whom were "rewarded tonight," and Chuck Roven for "putting the book in my hands." Nolan then thanks those who "believed in me my whole career," including Dan Michael, his brother Jonah, and his family. As he concludes, mentioning "the incredible Emma Thomas, producer," the audience, including Robert Downey Jr., Cillian Murphy, and Greta Gerwig, applauds warmly, with Jodie Foster visibly emotional and wiping tears from her eyes. The warm lighting emphasizes the grandeur of the event, underscoring the significance of this moment of collective appreciation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Christopher Nolan, dressed in a classic black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing at a podium on the Oscars stage, holding his golden Oscar statuette. He speaks with a mix of humility and emotion, stating, "Producer of all our films and all our children. I love you. Uh, and and uh, to the Academy, uh, just to say, uh, movies are just a little bit over 100 years old. I mean, imagine being there 100 years into painting or theater. We don't know where this incredible journey is going from here, but to know that you think that I'm a meaningful part of it means the world to me. Thank you very much." He occasionally glances down at the award and gestures subtly with his free hand, as the stage, adorned with deep purple drapes and a large golden Oscar statue, creates a regal and intimate atmosphere. Following his heartfelt speech, the audience erupts in applause. The camera then cuts to Robert Downey Jr., in a black tuxedo with a silver crescent-shaped lapel pin, clapping warmly, seated next to Greta Gerwig, who wears a sparkling silver beaded gown and smiles appreciatively. Martin Scorsese, in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, is also shown clapping with a serene expression. Upbeat, celebratory orchestral music begins to play. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the stage, now displaying the 'LEAD ACTRESS' category above a grand, golden, circular design with flowing drapes and floral arrangements. Five large vertical screens appear, each showing a different nominee holding an Oscar: one in a black floral dress, another in a teal gown, a third in a sparkling silver dress, a fourth in a black dress with a metallic skirt, and the fifth in a white gown with a high slit. An announcer's voice declares, "And the Oscar goes to... Sally Field, Jennifer Lawrence, Michelle Yeoh, Charlize Theron, Jessica Lange." The audience is shown standing and applauding as the nominees are displayed and then pan across the stage as they walk forward, with the audience continuing to cheer and clap, creating a celebratory and glamorous atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the grand, golden-themed Oscars stage, featuring flowing drapes and a large circular platform, as orchestral music swells and the audience erupts in applause and cheering. Several women in elegant gowns, including a Woman in White Dress and a Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress, walk onto the stage. The camera then focuses on the Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, who stands at the podium holding an envelope labeled 'ACTRESS IN A LEADING ROLE'. With a warm, expressive demeanor, she begins to speak, stating, "It's an honor for us to present the award for actress in leading role." As she continues, the large vertical screens behind her display the nominees, starting with the Woman in Yellow Dress, followed by the Woman in Plaid Shawl, the Woman in Gray Suit, the Woman in Blue-and-Black Top, and the Woman in Black Dress. The speaker specifically highlights "The amazing Sandra Hüller" (visually represented by the Woman in Yellow Dress) as nominated for "Anatomy of a Fall," describing her role as "a woman accused of killing her husband." She praises Hüller's performance, noting how she "maintains a certain ambiguity throughout, as if her character is capable of committing that crime," concluding with a playful "And here we are, Oscar night, and I still don't know." Throughout this, the audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, reacts with smiles and applause, and a split-screen shows her engaged in the audience alongside the smiling Woman in Black Sequined Blazer on stage. The atmosphere remains celebratory and formal, bathed in golden lighting, with a palpable sense of anticipation.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the audience, showing a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress with a diamond choker, smiling broadly and clapping amidst other formally dressed attendees, as the sound of enthusiastic applause fills the air. A distinct female voice is heard saying, "if she did it," followed by more applause. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the grand Oscars stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue, golden drapes, floral arrangements, and the illuminated 'LEAD ACTRESS' sign. Four women stand on a circular platform, while the applause subsides as the female voice continues. The camera cuts to a close-up of the woman with gray hair and glasses, wearing a flowing white gown with sheer sleeves, speaking with a warm, composed expression. She describes a character as "curious as a puppy and hungry as a bear," praising "Emma Stone's brilliant Bella" as "unhinged, uninhibited and completely original," highlighting her fearless entry into the world and awakening to life's sensations "without boundaries." She concludes by calling the character "unforgettable and endearing," created by an actor who is "always surprising" and "refuses to be categorized," before exclaiming, "Bravo, Emma!" The audience is shown again, with a woman in a strapless white gown and a diamond necklace with a yellow gem, smiling and looking up at the stage, seated next to a man in a black tuxedo, as laughter and applause erupt. The camera alternates between the speaking woman on stage and the audience, capturing their reactions, with the woman in the white gown laughing and clapping. The scene returns to the stage, where the woman in the white gown is joined by another woman in a black-and-white polka dot dress with a diamond necklace, who also speaks with a poised demeanor, addressing "Lily" and stating, "you are the soul of Killers of the Flower Moon," which is met with renewed applause. The lighting remains warm and golden, emphasizing the grandeur of the event, and the atmosphere is one of anticipation and celebration as the Lead Actress nominees are announced.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with close-ups of audience members at a formal awards ceremony, focusing first on a woman with long dark hair, wearing a navy blue strapless dress with a white cross-stitch pattern and a prominent blue gemstone necklace, who smiles warmly. The camera then cuts to a woman with blonde hair, wearing a black-and-white polka dot off-the-shoulder dress with a diamond necklace, who looks on with a composed expression. These two are briefly shown side-by-side in a split-screen format. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the stage, where four women stand in front of a large golden Oscar statue and a radiant golden backdrop, with the Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress among them. A woman with blonde hair pulled back, wearing a silver satin one-shoulder gown with a diamond choker and matching earrings, identified as the Woman in Silver Satin Gown, speaks clearly, praising a performance as "Molly Burkhart," noting it is "imbued with the plight, resilience, and spirit of the Osage people." She commends the "advocacy offset as a powerful display of dedication and leadership," before offering "Congratulations." This is met with enthusiastic applause and cheers from the audience. The camera then focuses on the Woman in Silver Satin Gown again as she calls out "Annette," prompting another wave of applause and cheers. She continues, sharing a personal anecdote: "I've never been stung by a jellyfish. But seeing you endure it multiple times made me feel like I had." She describes watching "Annette" portray "Diane Nyad" as an "intense sensory experience," where one can "taste the salt water, feel the sunburn, and relate to the relentless determination." During this, the audience is shown again, highlighting a woman with short blonde hair, wearing orange-tinted glasses, a dark blue sequined dress, and a sheer embroidered shawl, seated next to a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, both smiling and reacting. A split-screen returns, showing the Woman in Blue Sequined Dress with Orange Glasses on the left and the Woman in Silver Satin Gown on the right, both smiling and reacting to the stage. The warm, golden lighting emphasizes the glamour of the event, and the atmosphere is one of anticipation and celebration, punctuated by continuous applause.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen, showing the Woman in Blue Sequined Dress with Orange Glasses smiling and clapping on the left, while on the right, the Woman in Silver Satin Gown stands on stage, speaking with a composed expression. Her voice is heard delivering a heartfelt message: "Your work is a beautiful illustration of how to navigate the waves and the ocean and in life." The scene then transitions to a wide shot of the grand Oscars Stage, illuminated by the 'LEAD ACTRESS' sign above a golden backdrop featuring a massive Oscar statue. Four nominees, including the Woman in White Gown, the Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress, the Woman in Silver Satin Gown, and the Woman in Black Dress, stand poised on the circular stage amidst a swell of applause and uplifting orchestral music. The camera focuses on the Woman in Black Dress, who steps forward to announce the winner, her expression serious and professional. Her voice then begins to praise the nominee: "In Maestro, Carey Mulligan radiates as Leonard Bernstein's wife Felicia." The scene cuts to the audience, where the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Dress, identified as Carey Mulligan, seated next to the Man in White Tuxedo, reacts with a subtle smile and gentle claps, her expression shifting from anticipation to quiet satisfaction as the speaker continues: "Shining a piercing light on their complicated yet enduring love. But this level of excellence is the norm for Carey. For in every role she takes on, she infuses it with a unique humanity. Without this spectacular performance, Maestro would not have been the movie it became. Carey, you are so deserving of this honor." The video returns to a split-screen, showing Carey Mulligan looking thoughtful on the left, while the Woman in Black Dress on the right speaks with a warm, animated expression, gesturing slightly as she delivers the announcement. The warm, golden lighting throughout emphasizes the grandeur, and the audience's reactions convey a mix of excitement and emotional investment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Carey Mulligan, seated in the audience, visibly emotional and wiping her eyes as the 'LEAD ACTRESS' category is announced, accompanied by sustained, building applause. The camera then pulls back to reveal the grand Oscars stage, illuminated with golden drapes and a large 'LEAD ACTRESS' sign above. The presenter, a Woman in Black Sequined Blazer, stands at the podium holding the envelope, and the nominees are displayed on screen: Sandra Hüller, Emma Stone, Lily Gladstone, Annette Bening, and Carey Mulligan. The Woman in Black Sequined Blazer clearly announces, "And the Oscar goes to... Emma Stone." As Emma Stone is announced, seated in the audience, she erupts in joy, covering her mouth in disbelief, as loud cheers and triumphant orchestral music immediately erupt. She leaps up, hugging a Man in Black Tuxedo with a Red Boutonniere and then another Man in Black Tuxedo. She runs down the aisle, her strapless mint-green gown flaring as she moves, with the audience cheering and clapping, while the celebratory music continues. She reaches the stage, where she is handed the Oscar by the presenter. An announcer's voice states, "This is a second Oscar win and fifth nomination for Emma Stone. She took home her first Oscar for her leading performance in La La Land. She's the second woman to be nominated for acting and Best Picture for the same film." Emma then embraces a Woman in Black-and-White Polka Dot Dress, who is also on stage, before being joined by other nominees, including a Woman in Silver Satin Gown and a Woman in White Gown, as she holds the statuette and smiles triumphantly, all set against the backdrop of continuous applause and the grand orchestral music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Emma Stone, radiant in a strapless mint-green gown with a flared skirt and a diamond necklace featuring a yellow gem, rushing to the grand Oscars stage adorned with golden drapes and a large golden Oscar statue. Visibly emotional, she clutches her Oscar statuette and immediately quips, "Oh boy, my dress is broken. I think it happened during I'm just Ken," eliciting laughter and applause from the audience. Her voice, trembling with emotion, she admits, "Oh boy, this is really... this is really overwhelming. Sorry, I... okay, my voice is also a little gone. Whatever." As she speaks passionately into the microphone, gesturing expressively, the 'LEAD ACTRESS' sign illuminates above. She praises "the women on the stage" and "the women in this category," then directly addresses the nominees displayed on large vertical screens: "Sandra, Annette, Carey, Lily, I share this with you. I'm in awe of you, and it has been such an honor to do all of this together. I hope we get to keep doing more together." During this heartfelt speech, the camera cuts to the audience, showing emotional reactions, with Jodie Foster in a navy blue satin dress wiping tears and Carey Mulligan, seated in a black off-the-shoulder dress, also visibly moved. Emma continues, "Um, I don't know what I'm saying. Oh my god, I'm totally... okay. The other night, I was panicking, as you can kind of see. Happens a lot. Um, that maybe something like this could happen and and Yorgos said to me, 'Please...'" The celebratory and heartfelt atmosphere is palpable, emphasized by the warm, golden stage lighting.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video captures a triumphant moment at the Oscars as Emma Stone, wearing a strapless mint-green gown with a flared skirt and a diamond necklace featuring a yellow gem, rushes to the stage after winning the award for 'Actress in a Leading Role' for *Poor Things*. She is visibly emotional, clutching the Oscar statuette and speaking passionately into the microphone, gesturing expressively as she delivers her acceptance speech. The stage is adorned with golden drapes and a large golden Oscar statue in the background, with the category name 'LEAD ACTRESS' illuminated above. The nominees, including Carey Mulligan in a black-and-white polka dot dress, Lily Gladstone in a dark top, Sandra Hüller in a dark top, Annette Bening in a blue-and-black athletic-style top, and Emma Stone herself, are displayed on large vertical screens. As Emma speaks, the camera cuts to the audience, showing emotional reactions: Jodie Foster in a navy blue satin dress wipes tears, while Carey Mulligan, seated in a black off-the-shoulder dress, is also visibly moved. The atmosphere is celebratory and heartfelt, with Emma’s joy and gratitude radiating as she thanks her team and collaborators, her voice trembling with emotion. The stage lighting remains warm and golden, emphasizing the grandeur of the moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a bearded man in a black tuxedo, seated in the audience, looking intently toward the stage. The camera then cuts to Emma Stone, radiant in a mint-green strapless gown with a flared skirt and a diamond necklace featuring a yellow gem, standing at the Oscars Stage podium, holding her Oscar statuette. With an emotional and animated delivery, her voice slightly cracking, she passionately declares that "it's not about me," but "about a team that came together to make something greater than the sum of its parts," emphasizing that this collaborative spirit is "the best part about making movies." She expresses being "deeply honored to share this with every cast member, with every crew member, with every single person who poured their love and their care and their brilliance into the making of this film," specifically thanking Yorgos for "the gift of a lifetime in Bella Baxter." As she speaks, gesturing with her free hand, the background features golden drapes and a large circular stage design. The camera alternates between close-ups of Emma Stone and wide shots showing the engaged audience, including a bald man in a tuxedo with glasses and a woman in a black off-the-shoulder dress, both clapping and smiling, their applause audible in the background. A large screen above the stage displays a close-up of Da’Vine Joy Randolph in her emerald green gown, also smiling and clapping. The atmosphere is celebratory with warm lighting, and Emma, visibly moved, continues her heartfelt speech, acknowledging her family: "my mom, my brother Spencer, my dad, my husband Dave," and "most importantly, my daughter," as the audience remains engaged and appreciative, their applause swelling.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Emma Stone, radiant in a strapless mint-green gown with a flared skirt and a diamond necklace featuring a yellow gem, standing at the Oscars podium, holding her Oscar statuette. She delivers an emotional acceptance speech, her voice filled with gratitude as she speaks of her daughter, saying, "I love you bigger than the whole sky, my girl," and lightheartedly adds, "Don't look at the back of my dress." As uplifting orchestral music swells and applause fills the air, she gestures expressively before turning to embrace the other Lead Actress nominees—Sandra Hüller, Carey Mulligan, Lily Gladstone, and Annette Bening—all elegantly gowned and standing beside her on stage. The camera briefly cuts to the audience, showing Jodie Foster in a navy blue satin dress, visibly emotional and wiping tears, while others clap. A wide shot reveals the grand Oscars stage with golden drapes, a large golden Oscar statue, and 'OSCARS' illuminated above, all bathed in warm, golden light. The nominees then walk off stage together, smiling and hugging. The scene transitions to the Oscars Host, in a beige patterned tuxedo with a black bow tie, walking onto the stage with a confident stride. He speaks with a humorous and engaging voice, making a joke about the envelope, "Guys, make sure we tear up that envelope so there's no confusion with the best picture," which elicits audience laughter. He then continues his monologue with expressive hand movements, acknowledging the focus should be on "Emma and all these great actors and actresses and filmmakers," and mentions having "an extra minute" to share something he's proud of, before checking his phone as if reading from it. The celebratory and formal atmosphere is consistently emphasized by the warm lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Oscars Host, dressed in a beige patterned tuxedo with a black bow tie, standing on the warmly lit golden stage, intently checking his smartphone. He appears focused, occasionally glancing up and making small gestures, as if reading or preparing remarks. As he looks up, he begins to speak, gesturing expressively with his right hand while still holding the phone in his left, stating, "I just got a review..." He then humorously asks, "Has there ever been a worse host than Jimmy Kimmel at the Oscars?", eliciting immediate laughter from the audience. He continues to read a scathing "review" of Kimmel, describing his opening as "that of a less than average person trying too hard to be something which he is not and never can be," further amusing the crowd. The camera pulls back to reveal the grand Oscars Stage, featuring a massive golden Oscar statue flanked by pink blossoms and the word 'OSCARS' illuminated above. The host suggests replacing Kimmel with "another washed up but cheap ABC talent, George Stephanopoulos," which again draws laughter. He concludes the "review" with "He would make everybody on stage look bigger, stronger, and more glamorous. Blah blah blah, Make America Great Again," prompting a burst of laughter and applause from the audience, including visible reactions from Robert Downey Jr., Cillian Murphy, and Greta Gerwig. The host then challenges the audience to guess who posted the review on Truth Social, before revealing, "Well, thank you, President Trump." He then quips, "Thank you for watching. I'm surprised you're still... Isn't it past your jail time?", which causes the audience to erupt in loud, sustained laughter and enthusiastic applause, creating a lively and celebratory atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a smiling blonde woman in a black sequined strapless dress, laughing joyfully amidst the sound of loud applause and cheering, accompanied by fanfare-like orchestral music. The scene quickly transitions to the Oscars stage, where the Oscars Host, dressed in a beige patterned tuxedo with a black bow tie, walks confidently onto the stage, adjusting his bow tie and gesturing expressively as he begins his monologue, stating, "It's focused on the important stuff for sure." He then humorously asks, "Before we give out the final award, did anyone uh not get a chance to give a speech? We do have some time if you have anything you want to say. Just line up right here, we'll make sure everybody gets a chance to talk," eliciting audience laughter. The camera pulls back to reveal the grand Oscars Stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue flanked by pink floral arrangements and warm golden lighting, as the Host continues speaking with animated hand gestures, clapping and engaging the audience. He announces, "To present our final award of the evening, and to commemorate the 50th anniversary of The Godfather Part 2... a man who created some of the most iconic characters in movie history. He's an Oscar winner and a nine-time nominee, and we are so glad he was able to find a sitter so he could be here tonight. With the award for Best Picture, please welcome Al Pacino!" The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the auditorium, showing the Oscars Audience standing and applauding enthusiastically with continued cheering and the onset of intense, dramatic orchestral music, as Al Pacino, a man in a dark suit, walks across the stage toward the podium. The stage is illuminated with golden lights, and the audience is visibly enthusiastic, clapping and cheering as Pacino approaches the center of the stage, poised to deliver his acceptance speech. The atmosphere is celebratory and electric, with the golden hues of the stage and the audience’s energy creating a sense of grandeur and excitement, underscored by the powerful orchestral score.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the grand Oscars stage, bathed in warm golden light, featuring a large golden Oscar statue flanked by elegant pink floral arrangements. Tim Robbins, a man in a dark suit and glasses, walks confidently to the podium, holding a card and microphone. He begins his speech, greeting the audience with an emotional "Oh, my friends, thank you, thank you," as the Oscars Audience, including Robert Downey Jr., Cillian Murphy, and Greta Gerwig, claps, smiles, and laughs at his playful suggestion of doing Shakespeare, saying "to be... No, I'm not gonna do it." Robbins continues with animated gestures, announcing, "Well, this is the time, uh, for the last award of the evening," and expressing his honor to present it. He speaks with emotion, placing his hands on his chest, as he reveals that "ten wonderful films, uh, were nominated, but only one will take the award for Best Picture," holding up a card labeled 'BEST PICTURE'. The audience remains attentive as he builds suspense, stating, "And, uh, I have to go to the envelope for that. And I will," before revealing a golden envelope and declaring, "Here it comes," to a ripple of anticipation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Tim Robbins, dressed in a dark suit, standing at a podium on a golden-lit stage, holding a golden envelope and reading from a card, his clear voice announcing "And the Oscar is... Oppenheimer!" as enthusiastic applause immediately erupts. He affirms the win with a joyful "Yes! Yes!" and mentions "Emma Thomas, Charles Roven," followed by a lighthearted "What happened?" which elicits laughter from the audience, and a final "Wow." Triumphant orchestral music begins to swell as Christopher Nolan, in a black tuxedo, rises from his seat, holding his Oscar statuette, and begins walking down the aisle. He is followed by a group of cast and crew members, including Cillian Murphy, Robert Downey Jr., and Emma Stone, all in formal attire, clapping and celebrating. As they make their way through the audience, they are greeted with enthusiastic applause and hugs from fellow attendees, including Martin Scorsese, who is seen smiling and clapping. Amidst the continuous applause and swelling music, Tim Robbins continues to list Oppenheimer's impressive wins: "Oppenheimer has 13 nominations and has won seven Oscars tonight for Actor in a Supporting Role, Film Editing, Cinematography, Original Score, Actor in a Leading Role, Directing, and Best Picture." The group ascends the grand, golden-lit stage, which features a large golden Oscar statue backdrop and the words 'BEST PICTURE' illuminated above, gathering around the podium, embracing each other and sharing joyful moments. The audience continues to cheer with sustained, roaring applause as the winners celebrate their victory, with the camera capturing wide shots of the entire auditorium filled with clapping guests, all enveloped in an electric atmosphere of excitement and camaraderie.

segments.integrated_caption: The triumphant cast and crew of *Oppenheimer*, all in formal black-tie attire, gather on a grand circular stage adorned with golden drapes and pink floral arrangements, set against a massive golden Oscar statue. Emma Thomas, radiant in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves and green earrings, steps forward to the podium, clutching her Oscar statuette. With an emotional and joyful voice, she exclaims "Wow! Oh my goodness! Wow!" as the audience cheers loudly. She shares her long-held dream of this moment, admitting it seemed unlikely to ever happen, and now standing there, her mind has gone blank. Flanked by Charles Roven and Christopher Nolan, both in classic black tuxedos and holding their own Oscars, Emma declares that the reason the movie was what it was, was "Chris Nolan." The crowd erupts in cheers as she praises him as "singular" and "brilliant," expressing her deep gratitude to him. The on-screen graphic identifies the winners as 'EMMA THOMAS, CHARLES ROVEN, CHRISTOPHER NOLAN' for 'BEST PICTURE OPPENHEIMER'. Emma continues, emphasizing that the fantastic thing about their jobs is the collaboration and teamwork, thanking their "incredible" team, both cast and crew. The atmosphere is celebratory, with the audience's continuous clapping and cheering, and the stage bathed in warm golden light, highlighting the beaming faces of the group as the camera pans across them, capturing their shared elation and pride.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video captures the triumphant moment when the cast and crew of *Oppenheimer* accept the Best Picture Oscar on stage. The group, dressed in formal black-tie attire, gathers on a circular stage adorned with golden drapes and pink floral arrangements, with a massive golden Oscar statue as the backdrop. Emma Thomas, in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves and green earrings, steps forward to speak at the podium, holding her Oscar statuette. She is flanked by Charles Roven and Christopher Nolan, both in classic black tuxedos, each holding their own Oscars. The camera alternates between wide shots of the entire group and close-ups of Emma Thomas as she delivers an emotional, joyful speech, gesturing warmly and smiling broadly. The on-screen graphic identifies the winners as 'EMMA THOMAS, CHARLES ROVEN, CHRISTOPHER NOLAN' for 'BEST PICTURE OPPENHEIMER'. The atmosphere is celebratory, with the audience clapping and the stage bathed in warm golden light, emphasizing the grandeur of the moment. The group stands together, beaming, as the camera pans across their faces, capturing their shared elation and pride.

segments.integrated_caption: The video captures a triumphant moment on the grand Oscars stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue backdrop flanked by pink floral arrangements and a circular illuminated platform, all bathed in warm golden lighting. Emma Thomas, in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves and green earrings, delivers an emotional acceptance speech, her voice filled with gratitude and a touch of humor. Holding an Oscar statuette at the podium, she thanks the crew, Universal, IMAX (specifically Rich Gelfond and everyone else at IMAX) for believing in the movie, and all the theaters, stating that as a moviegoer and filmmaker, 'that's why we do what we do.' She then emotionally thanks her children, Flora, Rory, Oliver, and Magnus (who is not present), and acknowledges UK Mother's Day by remembering her late mother and thanking her mother-in-law Christina, who is present with her dad. Finally, she exclaims her honor and thanks the Academy, prompting a wave of loud, sustained applause and cheering from the audience. Flanked by Christopher Nolan, who smiles warmly in his black tuxedo and holds his own Oscar, and Charles Roven, also in a tuxedo and holding an Oscar, the group behind them, including a bald man with a gray beard and glasses, all in formal attire and some holding Oscars, celebrates the win. As the audience continues to cheer, Christopher Nolan then steps up to the microphone, beginning his own thanks to the Academy in a calm, clear voice.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video captures a triumphant moment on the Oscars stage as Emma Thomas, in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves and green earrings, delivers an emotional acceptance speech while holding an Oscar statuette. She stands at the podium with a microphone, flanked by Christopher Nolan, who is in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and holding his own Oscar, and Charles Roven, also in a tuxedo, holding an Oscar. The group, including other cast and crew members, stands behind them in formal attire, some holding Oscars, as they celebrate the win. The stage is grand, featuring a large golden Oscar statue backdrop with pink floral arrangements on either side, and a circular illuminated platform. Emma Thomas speaks with visible emotion, gesturing occasionally, while the audience applauds. The camera alternates between close-ups of her expressive face and wider shots of the entire group, capturing their joy and pride. Christopher Nolan smiles warmly, and Charles Roven looks on with pride. The atmosphere is celebratory and formal, with warm golden lighting enhancing the prestige of the moment. The group eventually claps and embraces, sharing the victory as the audience continues to cheer.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens on the grand Oscars stage, adorned with a massive golden Oscar statue backdrop and flanked by cascading pink floral arrangements, bathed in warm golden light. At the center of the circular, illuminated platform, Martin Scorsese, in a black tuxedo and his signature thick-rimmed glasses, holds a golden Oscar statuette and speaks into a microphone with an earnest and emotional expression. He thanks Universal Pictures, Donna Langley, and their team, as the audience, including Robert Downey Jr. smiling warmly and Jodie Foster visibly emotional, claps. Scorsese continues, expressing gratitude to his family and his wife, whom he affectionately calls the 'secret weapon' of his life. He then thanks James Woods and David Wargo for entrusting him with the project's book, which he was 'fortunate enough to give to Chris and Emma.' Christopher Nolan, in a classic black tuxedo, stands beside Scorsese, holding his own Oscar, listening attentively and occasionally glancing at Scorsese with a proud smile. Behind them, Emma Thomas, in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves, stands with other formally dressed cast and crew members, all holding Oscars and smiling with pride, including a bald man with a gray beard in a tuxedo and a woman in a one-shoulder sequined black dress. Scorsese concludes by reflecting on his 'amazing 20-plus year experience' with 'Chris and Emma,' highlighting their 'five movies over 20 years' and calling them a 'fantastic team,' stating that being part of making movies with them has been 'completely thrilling.' The camera alternates between wide shots of the celebratory group and close-ups of Scorsese, emphasizing the shared triumph and deep respect among these legendary filmmakers and their team.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video captures a moment from the Oscars ceremony on a grand stage adorned with a massive golden Oscar statue backdrop and flanked by cascading pink floral arrangements. At the center of the circular, illuminated stage, Christopher Nolan, in a classic black tuxedo with a bow tie, stands beside Martin Scorsese, who is also in a black tuxedo and wearing his signature thick-rimmed glasses. Both hold golden Oscar statuettes, with Scorsese speaking into a microphone while Nolan listens attentively, occasionally glancing at him with a proud smile. Behind them, a group of formally dressed individuals—cast and crew—stand in a line, including Emma Thomas in a shimmering black sequined gown with sheer polka-dotted sleeves and green earrings, and others in elegant gowns and tuxedos. The camera alternates between wide shots of the group and close-ups of Scorsese as he delivers his speech, his expression earnest and emotional. The lighting is warm and golden, emphasizing the celebratory and prestigious atmosphere. The audience is visible in the foreground of some wide shots, clapping and watching intently. The scene conveys a moment of shared triumph and respect between two legendary filmmakers, with the entire team standing in solidarity behind them.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of formally dressed individuals on the grand Oscars stage, bathed in golden light. A bald man with a gray beard, wearing a black tuxedo with glasses and holding an Oscar statuette, is heard saying "Thank you so much" amidst a burst of enthusiastic applause and cheering from the audience. Beside him, another man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie also holds an Oscar, celebrating their win. The camera pulls back to reveal the magnificent Oscars stage, featuring a large golden Oscar statue backdrop flanked by elegant pink floral arrangements and a circular illuminated platform, as the audience continues to clap loudly. The scene briefly cuts to the audience, showing Robert Downey Jr. smiling and clapping, followed by Carey Mulligan also applauding. The Oscars Host, a man in a beige patterned tuxedo with a black bow tie, then walks onto the stage with expressive gestures, clapping and waving to the audience. He speaks animatedly, using hand movements to emphasize his words, congratulating "Oppenheimer" and making a joke about Christopher Nolan texting his family, which elicits laughter from the audience. The Host continues, thanking the Academy, producers Raj Kapoor, Kadeen Milan, and Molly McGeary, announcer David Allen Greer, band leader Ricky Minor, director Hamish Hamilton, and the entire Oscars crew. He promotes "Abbott Elementary" and his show with Dwayne Johnson, Justin Timberlake, and Robert De Niro, before thanking viewers for watching the "96th Oscars" and bidding "Goodnight!" to continued applause and cheering. The segment concludes with a graphic overlay identifying Dave Mullins and Brad Booker, seen holding an Oscar, as the winners for 'ANIMATED SHORT FILM' for 'WAR IS OVER! INSPIRED BY THE MUSIC OF JOHN & YOKO'. The atmosphere remains celebratory and formal throughout.
