segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, celebratory orchestral score and crowd applause, as a male narrator's voice begins, stating, 'During the show, Oscars were also presented for...' This voice-over then accompanies a rapid succession of Oscar winners appearing on stage, each framed within a golden border against a textured gold background. Dave Mullins and Brad Booker, both in black tuxedos, are seen accepting the Animated Short Film award for 'War Is Over! Inspired by the Music of John & Yoko,' as the narrator explicitly names this category and film. Next, Nadia Stacey in a shimmering silver dress, Mark Coulier, and Josh Weston, all in formal attire, accept the Makeup and Hairstyling award for 'Poor Things,' with the narrator confirming 'Makeup and Hairstyling, Poor Things.' James Price in a black tuxedo and Shona Heath in a light green coat with a red scarf accept the Production Design award for 'Poor Things,' as the narrator states 'Production Design, Poor Things.' Holly Waddington, in a dark, embellished gown, accepts the Costume Design award for 'Poor Things,' with the narrator announcing 'Costume Design, Poor Things.' Takashi Yamazaki, Kiyoko Shibuya, Masaki Takahashi, and Tatsuji Nojima, all in formal wear, accept the Visual Effects award for 'Godzilla Minus One,' as the narrator says 'Visual Effects, Godzilla Minus One.' Jennifer Lame, in a green strapless gown, accepts the Film Editing award for 'Oppenheimer,' coinciding with the narrator's announcement 'Film Editing, Oppenheimer.' Ben Proudfoot in a black tuxedo and Kris Bowers in a light blue dress accept the Documentary Short Film award for 'The Last Repair Shop,' as the narrator states 'Documentary Short Film, The Last Repair Shop.' Mstyslav Chernov, Michelle Mizner, and Raney Aronson-Rath, in formal black attire, accept the Documentary Feature Film award for '20 Days in Mariupol,' with the narrator announcing 'Documentary Feature, 20 Days in Mariupol.' Hoyte van Hoytema, in a black tuxedo, accepts the Cinematography award for 'Oppenheimer,' as the narrator says 'Cinematography, Oppenheimer.' This is followed by a brief visual clip from 'The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar' showing a man in a suit running through a grand, sunlit hall, while the narrator announces the 'Live Action Short Film, The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar.' Finally, Tarn Willers in a black suit with a hat and Johnnie Burn in a black tuxedo accept the Sound award for 'The Zone of Interest,' with the narrator stating 'Sound, The Zone of Interest.' The segment concludes with end credits rolling over the gold background, listing Executive Producers, Co-Executive Producer, Director, and writers, all set against the continuing celebratory music and fading applause.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment presents a rapid succession of Oscar winners accepting their awards on stage, each framed within a golden border against a textured gold background. The sequence begins with Dave Mullins and Brad Booker, both in black tuxedos, accepting the Animated Short Film award for 'War Is Over! Inspired by the Music of John & Yoko,' with Mullins holding the Oscar and Booker standing beside him. Next, Nadia Stacey, Mark Coulier, and Josh Weston, dressed in formal attire, accept the Makeup and Hairstyling award for 'Poor Things,' with Stacey in a shimmering silver dress and Coulier in a black tuxedo. James Price and Shona Heath, in a black tuxedo and a light green coat with a red scarf respectively, accept the Production Design award for 'Poor Things.' Holly Waddington, in a dark, embellished gown, accepts the Costume Design award for 'Poor Things.' Takashi Yamazaki, Kiyoko Shibuya, Masaki Takahashi, and Tatsuji Nojima, in formal wear, accept the Visual Effects award for 'Godzilla Minus One.' Jennifer Lame, in a green strapless gown, accepts the Film Editing award for 'Oppenheimer.' Ben Proudfoot and Kris Bowers, in a black tuxedo and a light blue dress respectively, accept the Documentary Short Film award for 'The Last Repair Shop.' Mstyslav Chernov, Michelle Mizner, and Raney Aronson-Rath, in formal black attire, accept the Documentary Feature Film award for '20 Days in Mariupol.' Hoyte van Hoytema, in a black tuxedo, accepts the Cinematography award for 'Oppenheimer,' followed by a brief clip from 'The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar' showing a man in a suit running through a grand, sunlit hall. Tarn Willers and Johnnie Burn, in a black tuxedo and a black suit with a hat respectively, accept the Sound award for 'The Zone of Interest.' The segment concludes with end credits rolling over the gold background, listing Executive Producers Katy Mulan and Raj Kapoor, Co-Executive Producer Rob Paine, Director Hamish Hamilton, and a long list of writers including Jimmy Kimmel, Carol Leifer, and Molly McNearney.
